The CMS Chronic Conditions Data Warehouse (CCW) applies standardized algorithms to Medicare fee-for-service (FFS) claims (inpatient, outpatient, carrier, SNF, home health, hospice, and DME) to identify chronic conditions. CCW outputs are available for all Medicare beneficiaries regardless of age. However, since the algorithms rely solely on FFS claims, condition ascertainment is effectively limited to the FFS population. Beneficiaries always in Medicare Advantage (HMO) have no condition information, and those with partial HMO enrollment only contribute during their FFS months.

Access and Usage

These datasets are restricted-use due to the inclusion of sensitive and identifiable health information. As such, the data files themselves cannot be shared via this Dataverse entry.

Students, post-docs, and researchers conducting NSAPH-related research in collaboration with PIs at Harvard or affiliated institutions may gain access to the data, provided they have submitted a project initiation form, are listed on an approved IRB protocol, and have completed all required data security trainings.

Note: No data files are included in this entry. This documentation serves as a reference to the analytical data files generated from the raw CCW chronic conditions data CCW Chronic Conditions .

title: Atmospheric Composition Analysis Group (ACAG) North American Regional Estimates with Composition
author: Other
description: The Atmospheric Composition Analysis Group (ACAG) North American Regional Estimates with Composition (V4.NA.03) dataset presents estimated annual average fine particulate matter (PM2.5) air pollution total and compositional mass concentrations. This dataset covers North America with 0.01 degree (~1 km) spatial resolution during 2000 to 2018. Concentrations are estimated using satellite-based air pollution observations combined with a chemical transport model and subsequent calibration to ground-based air pollution observations using geographically weighted regression. This dataset covers total PM2.5 and the following PM2.5 components: black carbon (BC), ammonium (NH4), nitrate (NO3), sulfate (SO4), organic matter, mineral dust, and sea salt. This dataset is intended for use in large-scale, long-term epidemiological studies and health impact assessments.

title: Atmospheric Composition Analysis Group (ACAG) Global/Regional Estimates
author: Other
description: The Atmospheric Composition Analysis Group (ACAG) Global/Regional Estimates (V5.GL.04) dataset presents estimated monthly and annual average concentrations of fine particulate matter (PM2.5) air pollution. The dataset provides global coverage during 1998 to 2022 with 0.01 degree (~1 km) and 0.1 degree (~10 km) spatial resolution. Summary data tables are also available at the national scale for all countries and at the sub-national scale (e.g., for states, provinces, or regions) for Canada, China, India, and the United States. Concentrations are estimated using satellite-based air pollution observations combined with a chemical transport model and subsequent calibration to ground-based air pollution observations using geographically weighted regression. This dataset can be used to study monthly or seasonal variations in PM2.5 concentrations and is intended for use in large-scale epidemiological studies and health impact assessments.

title: Center for Air, Climate, and Energy Solutions (CACES) Empirical Model
author: Environmental Protection Agency (EPA)
description: The Center for Air, Climate, & Energy Solutions (CACES) Empirical Model (v1) dataset provides estimated annual average, population-weighted concentrations of criteria air pollutants for states, counties, Census tracts, and Census block groups in the contiguous United States during 1979 to 2020. This dataset is based on a land use regression model that incorporates data from ground-based regulatory monitors, satellite-based observations, and land use and land cover information. This dataset covers the following criteria air pollutants: particulate matter (PM2.5 and PM10), ozone (O3), sulfur dioxide (SO2), carbon monoxide (CO), and nitrogen dioxide (NO2). The CACES Empirical Model v1 dataset is intended to support various applications in public health, risk assessment, planning, and policy, and has been used in health effects and health disparities research.

title: Airport Data and Information Portal
author: Other
description: The U.S. Federal Aviation Administration (FAA) Airport Data and Information Portal (ADIP) is a comprehensive resource for accessing a wide range of airport-related data and information managed by the FAA. The portal provides essential information for various stakeholders, including airport operators, planners, engineers, and researchers, and supports various uses, such as designing new facility expansions, ensuring regulatory compliance, and assessing environmental impacts. Specifically, the ADIP provides location information (geographic coordinates) for all aircraft landing facilities (airports, heliports, balloonports, seaplane bases, gliderports, ultralight ports) in the United States and United States Territories during 1981 to 2023.

title: Federal Communications Commission (FCC) Cellular Towers
author: Other
description: The Cellular Towers dataset provides the locations of all cellular towers as recorded by the U. S. Federal Communications Commission (FCC). Cellular tower locations are provided as geographic coordinates (point locations). This dataset covers the United States and United States Territories in 2021. The dataset is intended for general planning, analytical, and research purposes.

title: Global Roads Open Access Data Set
author: National Aeronautics and Space Administration (NASA)
description: The Global Roads Open Access Data Set Version 1 (gROADSv1) provides road information with global coverage. This dataset harmonizes road data available from public sources for each country. The dataset is a composite of road information during 1980 to 2010, depending on the available data for each country. The gROADSv1 dataset provides road locations (lines) and their harmonized functional classifications (highway, primary road, secondary road, tertiary road, local road, trail, private road).

title: Toxic Release Inventory (TRI) Basic Data Files
author: Environmental Protection Agency (EPA)
description: The Toxic Release Inventory (TRI) Basic Data Files provide annual estimated toxic releases (i.e., pollutant mass emissions to air, water, and land) for specific pollutants that are required by regulations to be reported to the U. S. Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) by federal and industrial facilities. This includes industries involved in manufacturing, metal mining, electric power generation, chemical manufacturing, or hazardous waste treatment. The inventory includes chemicals that may cause cancer, significant adverse acute or chronic human health effects, or significant adverse environmental effects. The TRI covers the United States and United States Territories during 1987 to 2023. The TRI provides the point locations (geographic coordinates) of polluting industrial facilities and their facility-reported annual estimated mass emissions by pollutant and release type (air, water, or land). The TRI includes more than 800 pollutants (e.g., benzene, ethylbenzene, xylene, toluene).

title: Di et al. (2020) Daily and Annual NO2 Concentrations for the Contiguous United States, 1-km Grids, v1.10 (2000 – 2016)
author: Environmental Protection Agency (EPA)
description: The Daily and Annual NO2 Concentrations for the Contiguous United States, 1-km Grids, Version 1.10 (2000-2016) dataset contains nitrogen dioxide (NO2) air pollution concentration estimates. NO2 concentrations are estimated using an ensemble modeling approach with three machine learning models (neural network, random forest, and gradient boosting) and a generalized additive model. The approach incorporates satellite-based observations of NO2 column concentrations, land use and meteorological variables, chemical transport models, and other data sources. This dataset provides daily and annual average NO2 concentration estimates during 2000 to 2016 for the contiguous United States with 1 km spatial resolution.

title: Requia et al. (2020) Daily 8-Hour Maximum and Annual O3 Concentrations for the Contiguous United States, 1-km Grids, v1.10 (2000 – 2016)
author: Environmental Protection Agency (EPA)
description: The Daily 8-Hour Maximum and Annual O3 Concentrations for the Contiguous United States, 1-km Grids, Version 1.10 (2000-2016) dataset contains estimated ozone (O3) air pollution concentrations. O3 concentrations are estimated using a geographically weighted ensemble modeling approach with three types of machine learning models (neural network, random forest, and gradient boosting). The approach incorporates O3 air pollution observations from ground-based monitors, land use and meteorological variables, chemical transport models, and remote sensing data, along with other sources. This dataset provides daily 8-hour maximum average and annual 8-hour maximum average O3 concentration estimates during 2000 to 2016 for the contiguous United States with 1 km spatial resolution.

title: Wei et al. (2022)   Daily and Annual PM2.5, O3, and NO2 Concentrations at ZIP Codes for the Contiguous United States, v1  (2000 – 2016)
author: National Aeronautics and Space Administration (NASA)
description: The Daily and Annual PM2.5, O3, and NO2 Concentrations at ZIP Codes for the Contiguous U.S., 2000-2016, v1.0 dataset contains predictions of air pollution concentrations at the ZIP Code-level for 2000-2016. An ensemble framework consisting of three machine-learning models (Random Forest, Gradient Boosting, and Neural Network) was implemented to estimate the daily concentrations of fine particulate matter (PM2.5), ozone (O3), and nitrogen dioxide (NO2) in grid cells at 1-km resolution. Prediction variables included air monitoring data, satellite aerosol optical depth, meteorological conditions, chemical transport model simulations, and land-use variables. Annual predictions represent the average daily predictions in each grid cell for each year.

title: US Environmental Protection Agency Fused Air Quality Surface Using Downscaling (EPA FAQSD)
author: Environmental Protection Agency (EPA)
description: The U.S. Environmental Protection Agency, Fused Air Quality Surface Using Downscaling (EPA FAQSD) presents modeled PM2.5 (daily average) and O3 (daily 8-hr maximum) surfaces. Predictions are generated from a Bayesian space-time downscaling fusion model that uses both air quality monitoring data from the National Air Monitoring Stations/State and Local Air Monitoring Stations and numerical output data from the EPA Community Multiscale Air Quality (CMAQ) database. Data are available at the 2010 census tract level for 2002-2016.

title: Brokamp (2022) Daily PM2.5 Model
author: National Institutes of Health (NIH)
description: The study by Brokamp (2022) introduces a high-resolution spatiotemporal model for evaluating exposure to fine particulate matter (PM2.5) across the contiguous United States. The model provides a detailed analysis of fine particulate matter exposure, which is crucial due to its significant impact on public health, particularly in relation to respiratory and cardiovascular diseases. By offering a comprehensive assessment of fine particulate matter exposure at a granular level, this research provides valuable insights for environmental and public health policymakers to develop targeted interventions and regulations to mitigate the adverse effects of air pollution on human health.

title: Brokamp et al. (2019) Community Deprivation Index
author: National Institutes of Health (NIH)
description: The Community Deprivation Index (CDI) dataset contains a deprivation index for each census tract in the United States based on a principal components analysis of six different 2015 American Community Survey measures: population below poverty level, median income, high school education, health insurance status, assistance income, and vacant housing. The first component, poverty level, explains over 60% of the total variance compared to the other five measures. It is referred to as the “Deprivation Index”. Rescaling and normalization were used to scale the index to a range from 0 to 1, with 1 representing the most deprivation.

title: US Census TIGER/Line Shapefiles Primary and Secondary Roads (MAF/TIGER Feature Class Codes S1100 and S1200)
author: United States Census Bureau (US Census)
description: The US Census Bureau's TIGER/Line Shapefiles provide detailed geographic data for various features, including primary and secondary roads. These shapefiles are part of the MAF/TIGER (Master Address File/Topologically Integrated Geographic Encoding and Referencing) database and are essential for a wide range of mapping and geospatial analyses. TIGER/Line Shapefiles provide detailed vector data for geographic features across the United States. The Primary Roads (S1100) and Secondary Roads (S1200) shapefiles contain data about road networks, which is essential for transportation planning, geospatial analysis, and mapping.

title: US National Aeronautics and Space Administration (NASA) Terra Moderate Resolution Imaging Spectroradiometer (MODIS) Vegetation Indices (MOD13Q1), Version 6.1
author: National Aeronautics and Space Administration (NASA)
description: The MOD13Q1 Vegetation Indices is part of the Moderate Resolution Imaging Spectroradiometer (MODIS) suite of data products. It is designed to provide consistent and spatially detailed information on global vegetation conditions at a 250 m spatial resolution with a 16-day revisit period. The primary vegetation indices are the Normalized Difference Vegetation Index and the Enhanced Vegetation Index, which are used to monitor vegetation health and productivity.

title: Atmospheric Composition Analysis Group (ACAG) North American Regional Estimates with Composition
author: Other
description: The Atmospheric Composition Analysis Group (ACAG) North American Regional Estimates with Composition (V4.NA.03) dataset presents estimated annual average fine particulate matter (PM2.5) air pollution total and compositional mass concentrations. This dataset covers North America with 0.01 degree (~1 km) spatial resolution during 2000 to 2018. Concentrations are estimated using satellite-based air pollution observations combined with a chemical transport model and subsequent calibration to ground-based air pollution observations using geographically weighted regression. This dataset covers total PM2.5 and the following PM2.5 components: black carbon (BC), ammonium (NH4), nitrate (NO3), sulfate (SO4), organic matter, mineral dust, and sea salt. This dataset is intended for use in large-scale, long-term epidemiological studies and health impact assessments.

title: US Multi-Resolution Land Characteristics Consortium (MRLC) National Land Cover Database (NLCD), Imperviousness All Years (CONUS)
author: Other
description: The National Land Cover Database (NLCD) includes the Imperviousness All Year dataset. It provides specific data for impervious surfaces across the contiguous United States (CONUS) and is managed by the U.S. Multi-Resolution Land Characteristics Consortium (MRLC). The data provide critical information about the extent and distribution of impervious surfaces as well as estimates of impervious surfaces, such as roads, buildings, and other man-made structures that prevent water from infiltrating the ground. The data are expressed as a percentage of imperviousness for each 30-meter pixel.

title: US Multi-Resolution Land Characteristics Consortium (MRLC) National Land Cover Database (NLCD), Land Cover All Years (CONUS)
author: Other
description: The National Land Cover Database (NLCD) is a comprehensive land cover database for the contiguous United States (CONUS) that is managed by the Multi-Resolution Land Characteristics (MRLC) Consortium. One specific datasets within the NLCD is the Land Cover (LC) All Years product. Land cover classifications are derived from satellite-based imagery and are provided every 2-3 years starting in 2001 at 30 m resolution for the CONUS.

title: US Multi-Resolution Land Characteristics Consortium (MRLC) National Land Cover Database (NLCD), Tree Canopy Cover All Years (CONUS)
author: Other
description: The National Land Cover Database (NLCD) is a comprehensive land cover database for the contiguous United States (CONUS) that is managed by the Multi-Resolution Land Characteristics (MRLC) Consortium. One specific dataset within the NLCD is the Tree Canopy (TC) Cover All Years product. It provides estimates of tree canopy density across the CONUS and represents the percentage of land area that is covered by tree canopies.

title: US Department of Transportation (DOT), Federal Highway Administration (FHWA), Highway Performance Monitoring System (HPMS)
author: Other
description: The Federal Highway Administration (FHWA) manages the Highway Performance Monitoring System (HPMS), a comprehensive database of the United States highway system. HPMS provides data on the extent, condition, performance, use, and operating characteristics of the nation's highways.

title: US Geological Survey (USGS) Global Multi-resolution Terrain Elevation Data 2010 (GMTED2010)
author: United States Geological Survey (USGS)
description: The Global Multi-resolution Terrain Elevation Data 2010 (GMTED2010) is a global digital elevation model developed collaboratively by the U.S. Geological Survey and the National Geospatial-Intelligence Agency. It provides elevation data that represents the Earth's surface topography and is derived from various aggregated data sources including satellite-based observations and land surveys. This enhanced the resolution and accuracy of the data and improved upon the previous GTOPO30 dataset.

title: US National Aeronautics and Space Administration (NASA) Terra Moderate Resolution Imaging Spectroradiometer (MODIS) Land Surface Temperature/Emissivity Daily product (MOD11A1), Version 6.1
author: National Aeronautics and Space Administration (NASA)
description: The MOD11A1 is part of the Moderate Resolution Imaging Spectroradiometer (MODIS) suite of data products. It is specifically designed to provide daily land surface temperature (LST) and emissivity data at a 1 km spatial resolution. The LST data are derived from thermal infrared observations made by the MODIS sensor, which are corrected for atmospheric effects and emissivity.

title: US National Aeronautics and Space Administration (NASA) Terra Moderate Resolution Imaging Spectroradiometer (MODIS) Level 2 Cloud Product (MOD06_L2), Version 6.1
author: National Aeronautics and Space Administration (NASA)
description: The MOD06_L2 is part of the Moderate Resolution Imaging Spectroradiometer (MODIS) suite of data products. It provides detailed information on various cloud properties retrieved from MODIS observations. These properties include cloud optical thickness, cloud effective radius, cloud top temperature, cloud top pressure, and cloud phase.

title: US National Aeronautics and Space Administration (NASA) Terra/Aqua Moderate Resolution Imaging Spectroradiometer (MODIS) Level 2 Land Aerosol Optical Depth Product (MCD19A2), Version 6.1
author: National Aeronautics and Space Administration (NASA)
description: The MCD19A2 is part of the Moderate Resolution Imaging Spectroradiometer (MODIS) suite of data products. It is specifically designed to provide aerosol optical depth (AOD) measurements over land. It uses the Multi-Angle Implementation of Atmospheric Correction algorithm to retrieve AOD data, which is crucial for understanding air quality, environmental changes, and atmospheric processes.

title: US National Aeronautics and Space Administration (NASA) Terra Moderate Resolution Imaging Spectroradiometer (MODIS) Level 2 Surface Reflectance Daily Product (MOD09GA), Version 6.1
author: National Aeronautics and Space Administration (NASA)
description: MOD09GA is part of the Moderate Resolution Imaging Spectroradiometer (MODIS) suite of data products. It specifically  provides estimates of the global surface spectral reflectance of MODIS bands 1-7 at 500 m and 1 km resolution. Observations are corrected for atmospheric conditions such as gases, aerosols, and Rayleigh scattering, which allows for more accurate measurements of surface properties.

title: US National Aeronautics and Space Administration (NASA) VIIRS/NPP Gap-Filled Lunar BRDF-Adjusted Nighttime Lights Daily L3 Global 500m Linear Lat Lon Grid (VNP46A2)
author: National Aeronautics and Space Administration (NASA)
description: Global daily measurements of nocturnal visible and near-infrared light, collected by Day-Night Band sensors in the Visible Infrared Imaging Radiometer Suite (VIIRS), are provided in this dataset at 500 m resolution. The ultra-sensitivity of the VIIRS sensors in lowlight conditions enables the generation of science-quality nighttime products that are suitable for Earth system science and applications due to substantial improvements in sensor resolution and calibration compared to the previous era  products.

title: US National Aeronautics and Space Administration (NASA) Global Modeling and Assimilation Office (GMAO) Modern-Era Retrospective Analysis for Research and Applications Version 2 (MERRA-2) inst3_3d_aer_Nv: 3-dimensional, 3-Hourly, Instantaneous, Model-Level, Assimilation, Aerosol Mixing Ratio (M2I3NVAER), Version 5.12.4
author: National Aeronautics and Space Administration (NASA)
description: An instantaneous 3-dimensional, 3-hourly data collection of aerosol mixing ratios is presented in the Modern-Era Retrospective Analysis for Research and Applications version 2 (MERRA-2). Global model and data assimilations of aerosol mixing ratio parameters at 72 model vertical layers are used to predict atmospheric aerosol mixing ratios every 3 hours.

title: US National Aeronautics and Space Administration (NASA) Global Modeling and Assimilation Office (GMAO) Modern-Era Retrospective analysis for Research and Applications Version 2 (MERRA-2) tavg1_2d_aer_Nx: 2-dimensional, 1-Hourly, Time-averaged, Single-Level, Assimilation, Aerosol Diagnostics  (M2T1NXAER), Version 5.12.4
author: National Aeronautics and Space Administration (NASA)
description: An hourly time-averaged 2-dimensional data collection of atmospheric aerosol parameters is provided in the Modern-Era Retrospective analysis for Research and Applications version 2 (MERRA-2). Global model and data assimilations that incorporate satellite-based observations are used to predict hourly aerosol parameters that include column mass density and surface-level mass concentrations of aerosol components, and total extinction (and scattering) aerosol optical thickness, at 550 nm.

title: US National Aeronautics and Space Administration (NASA) Global Modeling and Assimilation Office (GMAO) Modern-Era Retrospective analysis for Research and Applications Version 2 (MERRA-2) tavgM_2d_slv_Nx: 2-dimensional Monthly mean, Time-Averaged, Single-Level, Assimilation, Single-Level Diagnostics  (M2TMNXSLV), Version 5.12.4
author: National Aeronautics and Space Administration (NASA)
description: A monthly time-averaged 2-dimensional data collection of surface-level, meteorological parameters are available in the Modern-Era Retrospective analysis for Research and Applications version 2 (MERRA-2). It contains global model and data assimilation results that incorporate satellite-based observations to predict monthly, surface-level, atmospheric meteorological parameters such as air temperature, wind components, sea level pressure, surface pressure, and total precipitable water vapor.

title: US National Aeronautics and Space Administration (NASA) Global Modeling and Assimilation Office (GMAO) Modern-Era Retrospective analysis for Research and Applications Version 2 (MERRA-2) tavgM_2d_adg_Nx: 2-dimensional, Monthly mean, Time-averaged, Single-Level, Assimilation, Aerosol Diagnostics (extended)  (M2TMNXADG), Version 5.12.4
author: National Aeronautics and Space Administration (NASA)
description: A monthly time-averaged 2-dimensional data collection of additional (extended) atmospheric aerosol parameters is also available in Modern-Era Retrospective Analysis for Research and Applications version 2 (MERRA-2). It contains global model and data assimilation results that incorporate satellite-based observations to predict additional monthly aerosol parameters that include dry and wet deposition of aerosol components, dust emission and sedimentation by particle diameter bin, and organic carbon convective scavenging.

title: US National Aeronautics and Space Administration (NASA) Global Modeling and Assimilation Office (GMAO) Modern-Era Retrospective analysis for Research and Applications Version 2 (MERRA-2) tavgM_2d_flx_Nx: 2-dimensional, Monthly mean, Time-Averaged, Single-Level, Assimilation, Surface Flux Diagnostics   (M2TMNXFLX), Version 5.12.4
author: National Aeronautics and Space Administration (NASA)
description: A monthly time-averaged 2-dimensional data collection of surface flux diagnostics is available in the Modern-Era Retrospective analysis for Research and Applications version 2 (MERRA-2). It provides global model and data assimilation results that incorporate satellite-based observations to predict monthly surface (approx. 60 m above ground) flux diagnostics, that include total precipitation, bias corrected total precipitation, surface air temperature, surface specific humidity, surface wind speed, and evaporation from turbulence.

title: US National Aeronautics and Space Administration (NASA) Global Modeling and Assimilation Office (GMAO) Modern-Era Retrospective analysis for Research and Applications Version 2 (MERRA-2) MERRA-2 tavgM_2d_int_Nx: 2-dimensional, Monthly mean, Time-Averaged, Single-Level, Assimilation, Vertically Integrated Diagnostics (M2TMNXINT), Version 5.12.4
author: National Aeronautics and Space Administration (NASA)
description: A monthly time-averaged 2-dimensional data collection of water and energy related diagnostics is provided in the Modern-Era Retrospective analysis for Research and Applications version 2 (MERRA-2). It contains global model and data assimilation results that incorporate satellite-based observations to predict monthly water and energy related vertically integrated diagnostics, such as auto-conversion loss of cloud water, convective source of cloud ice, eastward (northward) flux of atmospheric ice (liquid, vapor), total potential energy tendency, and kinetic energy tendency.

title: US National Aeronautics and Space Administration (NASA) Global Modeling and Assimilation Office (GMAO) Modern-Era Retrospective analysis for Research and Applications Version 2 (MERRA-2) tavgM_2d_lnd_Nx: 2-dimensional, Monthly mean, Time-Averaged, Single-Level, Assimilation, Land Surface Diagnostics (M2TMNXLND), Version 5.12.4
author: National Aeronautics and Space Administration (NASA)
description: A monthly time-averaged 2-dimensional data collection of land surface parameters is presented in the Modern-Era Retrospective analysis for Research and Applications version 2 (MERRA-2). It provides global model and data assimilation results that incorporate satellite-based observations to predict monthly land surface parameters such as baseflow flux, runoff, surface soil wetness, root zone soil wetness, water at surface layer, water at root zone layer, and soil temperature.

title: US National Aeronautics and Space Administration (NASA) Global Modeling and Assimilation Office (GMAO) Modern-Era Retrospective analysis for Research and Applications Version 2 (MERRA-2) tavgM_2d_rad_Nx: 2-dimensional, Monthly mean, Time-Averaged, Single-Level, Assimilation, Radiation Diagnostics V5.12.4 (M2TMNXRAD), Version 5.12.4
author: National Aeronautics and Space Administration (NASA)
description: A monthly time-averaged 2-dimensional data collection of radiation parameters is included in the Modern-Era Retrospective analysis for Research and Applications version 2 (MERRA-2). It contains global model and data assimilation results that incorporate satellite-based observations to predict monthly radiation parameters, that include surface albedo, cloud area fraction, in cloud optical thickness, surface incoming shortwave flux (i.e., solar radiation), surface net downward shortwave flux, and upwelling longwave flux at top of atmosphere (i.e., outgoing longwave radiation).

title: Beck et al. (2023) Köppen-Geiger Global 1-km Climate Classification Maps
author: Other
description: The Beck et al. (2023) Köppen-Geiger Global 1-km Climate Classification Maps (Version 2) cover global land surfaces with spatial resolution of 0.01 degrees (~1 km). Additional maps are available for coarser spatial resolutions of 0.1 degrees, 0.5 degrees, and 1 degree. Maps cover six approximately 30-year time periods, including four historic periods (1901-1930, 1931-1960, 1961-1990, and 1991-2020) and two future time periods (2041-2070 and 2071-2099). The historic climate classifications are derived from multiple observation-based datasets of air temperature and precipitation. The future projected climate classifications are based on downscaled and bias-corrected air temperature and precipitation projections from the Coupled Model Intercomparison Project Phase 6 (CMIP6) for seven different socioeconomic scenarios (Shared Socioeconomic Pathway (SSPs)). Köppen-Geiger climate classifications include five major classes (tropical, arid, temperate, cold, and polar) and 30 sub-classes. The Köppen-Geiger climate classifications can be used for assessing environmental change, ecological modeling, and other applications that require information on variation in climate classification at local to global scales.

title: Gridded Population of the World (GPWv4) Population Density Adjusted to Match 2015 Revision United Nations World Population Prospects Country Totals
author: National Aeronautics and Space Administration (NASA)
description: The Gridded Population of the World (GPWv4) Population Density Adjusted to Match 2015 Revision United Nations World Population Prospects (UN WPP) Country Totals (Version 4.11) dataset presents global population density estimates at 5-year intervals during 2000 to 2020. The gridded population density estimates are available for multiple spatial resolutions for ease of integration with other gridded earth science and remote sensing datasets. The following spatial resolutions are available: 30 arcseconds (~1 km), 2.5 arcminutes, 15 arcminutes, 30 arcminutes, and 1 degree. The estimates are based on country-specific census and population registers and are adjusted to match the country population totals from the UN WPP. The GPWv4 population density dataset has been used in various applications combining population and environmental data, such as vulnerability mapping, disaster impact assessments, and research on environmental changes related to human health. The dataset is also intended for use in developing geospatial models.

title: Goddard Earth Observing System Composition Forecasting (GEOS-CF) Products
author: National Aeronautics and Space Administration (NASA)
description: Goddard Earth Observing System Composition Forecasting (GEOS-CF) Products Version 1.0 offers hourly air pollution composition and concentration predictions from a three-dimensional global chemical transport model. The model captures features such as spatio-temporal variations in air pollution due to changes in emissions, weather, and chemistry. Atmospheric observations are made at 25 km resolution.

title: US Geological Survey (USGS) Landsat Collection 2 Surface Reflectance-derived Spectral Indices
author: United States Geological Survey (USGS)
description: The Landsat Collection 2 Surface Reflectance-derived Spectral Indices offers data for vegetation, moisture, burn ratio, and snow indices that are derived from satellite-based observations of global surface reflectance. The data are available upon request and are generated at 30 m spatial resolution.

title: US Geological Survey (USGS) Landsat Collection 2 Surface Temperature
author: United States Geological Survey (USGS)
description: The Landsat Collection 2 Surface Temperature (L-C2-ST) presents surface temperature data derived from satellite-based thermal observations. The data are provided at 100 m spatial resolution.

title: US Geological Survey (USGS) Global Forest Cover Change (GFCC) Tree Cover Multi-Year Global 30 m Version 3 (GFCC30TCv003)
author: United States Geological Survey (USGS)
description: The Global Forest Cover Change (GFCC), Tree Cover Multi-Year Global dataset contains tree canopy land cover data for the contiguous United States. The data are derived from satellite-based land imagery and are provided every 5 years from 2000-2015 at 30 m resolution.

title: U.S. Air Force Defense Meteorological Satellite Program (DMSP) Operational Linescan System (OLS) Nighttime Lights Time Series, Version 4
author: Other
description: The Nighttime Lights Time Series dataset contains files of cloud-free composite images from all of the available archived U.S. Air Force Defense Meteorological Satellite Program (DMSP) Operational Line scan System. Global, smooth resolution, satellite-based, annual observations of persistent nighttime lights (e.g., lights from cities and towns, gas flares) at 1 km spatial resolution are available for calendar years 1992-2013.

title: Demuzere et al. (2022) Global Local Climate Zones (LCZ) Map, Version 3.0.0
author: Other
description: The Global Local Climate Zone (LCZ) Map is a 100 m-resolution world map of local climate zones. The is derived from multiple earth observation datasets of land cover and buildings and is composed of 10 built and 7 natural landscape types established by expert and crowd-sourced classification along with machine learning approaches. LCZ is intended for use as an indicator of local urban design with relevance for urban heat island identification.

title: CanClim North American Historical Monthly Spatial Climate Models
author: Other
description: The CanClim North American Historical Monthly Spatial Climate Models dataset contains monthly minimum/maximum air temperature and precipitation predictions for North America. The predictions were derived using spatial interpolation methods with ground-based monitoring station data that were collected at approximately 2 km spatial resolution from 1901 to 2016.

title: North American Land Data Assimilation System Phase 2 (NLDAS-2), Hourly Primary Forcing File A (NLDAS_FORA0125_H), Version 2.0
author: National Aeronautics and Space Administration (NASA)
description: The North American Land Data Assimilation System Phase 2, Hourly Primary Forcing File A (NLDAS-2_FORA0125_H) presents hourly gridded weather-related data predictions from 1979 to present. The data are derived from observations and model reanalysis of North American Regional Reanalysis data and presented at approximately 14 km spatial resolution. Predictions of near-surface air temperature, humidity, pressure, and precipitation are included. Data are available to search and download from the NLDAS-2_FORA0125_H dataset.

title: US Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC) National Environmental Public Health Tracking Network (NEPHTN) Indicator: Historical Temperature and Heat Index
author: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC)
description: The National Environmental Public Health Tracking Network (NEPHTN) Indicator: Historical Temperature and Heat Index dataset presents standardized, population-weighted, daily air temperature and other heat-related metrics. The data, derived from the North American Land Data Assimilation System predictions for 1979 to 2023, are aggregated to the state, county, and census tract level for the contiguous United States.

title: US Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC) National Environmental Public Health Tracking Network (NEPHTN) Indicator: Historical Precipitation
author: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC)
description: The National Environmental Public Health Tracking Network (NEPHTN), Indicator: Historical Precipitation dataset presents standardized, population-weighted daily precipitation metrics. The data, derived from the North American Land Data Assimilation System predictions for 1979 to 2023, are aggregated to the state, county, and census tract level for the contiguous United States.

title: US Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC) National Environmental Public Health Tracking Network (NEPHTN) Indicator: Vulnerability and Preparedness: Precipitation and Flooding
author: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC)
description: The National Environmental Public Health Tracking Network (NEPHTN) Indicator: Vulnerability and Preparedness: Precipitation and Flooding dataset presents standardized, county-level estimates of land, population, and housing units in designated flood hazard areas. The data are based on FEMA and EPA EnviroAtlas flood hazard maps of the contiguous United States.

title: US National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration (NOAA) National Weather Service (NWS) Storm Events Database
author: National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration (NOAA)
description: The Storm Event data for the United States are collected and standardized by the National Weather Service from a variety of sources, e.g., state government, local government, media reports. Storm event data from 1950 to 2022 are available and include type, location (county), dates, associated injuries and fatalities, and a narrative description of the event.

title: US Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) EnviroAtlas Estimated Floodplain Map for the Conterminous United States
author: Environmental Protection Agency (EPA)
description: The EnviroAtlas Estimated Floodplain dataset offers a 100-year flood inundation map for the contiguous United States with 30 m spatial resolution. A random forest statistical model was used to classify floodplains and develop the map using national land cover, soil, and elevation data. The model captured 79% of the 100-year floodplain identified by FEMA. The map is intended to fill gaps in spatial coverage of the FEMA Flood Insurance Rate Maps.

title: US Federal Emergency Management Agency (FEMA) National Flood Hazard Layer
author: Federal Emergency Management Agency (FEMA)
description: The National Flood Hazard Layer (NFHL) provides 100-year and 500-year flood inundation maps of the contiguous United States. The dataset was developed by FEMA to support the National Flood Insurance Program to help better understand the type and level of flooding risk for specific locations.

title: US Department of Agriculture (USDA) Risk Management Agency (RMA) AgRisk Viewer, Version 1.0
author: United States Department of Agriculture (USDA)
description: The United States Department of Agriculture (USDA), AgRisk Viewer displays publicly available crop loss insurances data obtained from the Risk Management Agency. Claims may include crop loss due to excess moisture/precipitation/rain, drought, hail, heat, cold wet weather, freeze, wind/excess wind, flood, among other factors.

title: Air Quality Data Pathfinder (AQ-Pathfinder)
author: National Aeronautics and Space Administration (NASA)
description: The Air Quality Data Pathfinder (AQ-Pathfinder) is one of several Data Pathfinders NASA designed to help guide users through the process of selecting and using application-specific datasets from NASA’s Earth science data collections. The datasets are acquired through collaboration with other federal entities and international space organizations from satellite, airborne, and ground-based instruments that monitor air quality. The resources available can aid with studies to forecast poor air quality, track trace gases, visualize air quality trends, and forecast the movement of pollutants. Links to the different data sources as well as visualization tools and other resources are also available.

title: Air Quality Data Pathfinder (AQ-Pathfinder)
author: National Aeronautics and Space Administration (NASA)
description: The Air Quality Data Pathfinder (AQ-Pathfinder) is one of several Data Pathfinders NASA designed to help guide users through the process of selecting and using application-specific datasets from NASA’s Earth science data collections. The datasets are acquired through collaboration with other federal entities and international space organizations from satellite, airborne, and ground-based instruments that monitor air quality. The resources available can aid with studies to forecast poor air quality, track trace gases, visualize air quality trends, and forecast the movement of pollutants. Links to the different data sources as well as visualization tools and other resources are also available.

title: Air Quality Data Pathfinder (AQ-Pathfinder)
author: National Aeronautics and Space Administration (NASA)
description: The Air Quality Data Pathfinder (AQ-Pathfinder) is one of several Data Pathfinders NASA designed to help guide users through the process of selecting and using application-specific datasets from NASA’s Earth science data collections. The datasets are acquired through collaboration with other federal entities and international space organizations from satellite, airborne, and ground-based instruments that monitor air quality. The resources available can aid with studies to forecast poor air quality, track trace gases, visualize air quality trends, and forecast the movement of pollutants. Links to the different data sources as well as visualization tools and other resources are also available.

title: Air Quality System (AQS)
author: Environmental Protection Agency (EPA)
description: The EPA's Air Quality System (AQS) is a comprehensive network of monitoring stations that collects and distributes data on various air pollutants. The monitoring stations provide hourly measurements of pollutants such as nitrogen dioxide (NO2), ozone (O3), and particulate matter (PM) (Lamsal et al., 2008). The data are considered the gold standard for measuring air pollutants (Considine et al., 2023). The EPA's efforts to improve air quality are guided by the Clean Air Act, which requires the agency to evaluate the costs and benefits of new rules and regulations to reduce pollution (McGuffey, 2016).

title: AirNow
author: Environmental Protection Agency (EPA)
description: AirNow is a comprehensive repository of air quality data collected by the U.S. EPA from thousands of monitoring stations across the U.S. and worldwide. It provides real-time and historical data based on the daily Air Quality Index (AQI) readings for key air pollutants in the Clean Air Act and is used by researchers, policymakers, and public health officials to support their initiatives. The data are also available to the public to help raise awareness of air pollution and its related health impacts. By providing accurate and up-to-date data, AirNow empowers individuals to make informed decisions regarding  outdoor activities to protect their health. AirNow partners include other federal agencies (e.g., NOAA, NASSA, CDC), U.S. tribal, state, and local agencies, and Canadian and Mexican partners.

title: Biosphere Data Pathfinder (Biosphere-Path)
author: National Aeronautics and Space Administration (NASA)
description: The Biosphere Data Pathfinder (Biosphere-Path) is one of several Data Pathfinders NASA designed to help guide users through the process of selecting and using application-specific datasets from NASA’s Earth science data collections. It has four components 1) Vegetation Characteristics and Processes Data, 2) Biodiversity-related Spectroscopy Data, 3) Human Impacts Data, and 4) Species Distribution Modeling. Together, they provide access to vegetation health (such as canopy height and evapotranspiration), spectroscopy, and species distribution modeling data, as well as data for monitoring human-induced changes that allow users to monitor biodiversity and forecast changes to ecological communities.

title: Diseases/Epidemics Data Pathfinder (DE-Pathfinder)
author: National Aeronautics and Space Administration (NASA)
description: The Diseases/Epidemics Data Pathfinder (DE-Pathfinder) is one of several Data Pathfinders NASA designed to help guide users through the process of selecting and using application-specific datasets from NASA’s Earth science data collections. It is the result of collaboration with other federal entities and international space organizations to provide information to help understand how environmental changes can lead to disease emergence, transmission, and outbreaks. Remote sensing data are used to map, monitor, and predict areas or regions at risk for disease outbreaks. Satellite imagery, coupled with ground-based data, aids in understanding many natural phenomena and human behaviors. The data products, which are openly available, have been used to understand disease outbreaks such as malaria, Rift Valley fever, and COVID-19, and how changes in human behavior are having impacts on the environment. The World Health Organization notes that disease outbreaks are often associated with environmental conditions. Changes in water and air quality, temperature, and precipitation for example, can affect disease transmission.

title: EnviroAtlas
author: Environmental Protection Agency (EPA)
description: EnviroAtlas is an online mapping and analysis tool developed by the U.S. EPA to provide public access to geospatial data and resources related to environment and human health. The primary purpose of EnviroAtlas is to support informed decision-making and promote a better understanding of the relationships between ecosystems, their services, and human well-being. It provides access to data at different spatial scales for environmental indicators such as air quality, water quality, land use, and ecosystem services through its interactive map, data downloads, tools, and other resources.

title: Fire Information for Resource Management System (FIRMS)
author: National Aeronautics and Space Administration (NASA)
description: The Fire Information for Resource Management System (FIRMS) distributes Near Real-Time active fire data from the Moderate Resolution Imaging Spectroradiometer (MODIS) aboard the Aqua and Terra satellites, and the Visible Infrared Imaging Radiometer Suite (VIIRS) aboard S-NPP and NOAA 20 (formally known as JPSS-1). Globally these data are available within 3 hours of satellite observation, but for the U.S. and Canada active fire detections are available in real-time.

title: FIREX FireLab 2016 (FIREX)
author: National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration (NOAA)
description: FIREX FireLab 2016 (FIREX) is a product of the NOAA Chemical Sciences Laboratory (CSL) that focuses on measuring fuels or combustion conditions that are characteristic of the western U.S. that may be under-sampled by the fire research community. Data from measurements of gas and particle phase emissions and processing from FIREX are freely available to the public and scientific community to support technology transfer, inform decision makers, and contribute to assessments and reports. Emission products analyzed include SVOCs, VOCs, Black Carbon, and Brown Carbon, to name a few.

title: Landslides Data Pathfinder
author: National Aeronautics and Space Administration (NASA)
description: The Landslides Data Pathfinde is one of several Data Pathfinders NASA designed to help guide users through the process of selecting and using application-specific datasets from NASA’s Earth science data collections. It is designed for researchers, decision-makers, and the public to help better understand the interaction of complex events that cause landslides. The data, available from the datasets and tools provided, include information about past landslides, erosion, and fluvial processes in sloped areas. It includes data from various agency members of the Committee on Earth Observation Satellites. The information is used to help monitor and mitigate landslide hazards.

title: GOESR Advanced Baseline Imaging Products (GRABIPRD)
author: National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration (NOAA)
description: The Geostationary Operational Environmental Satellites R-series (GOES-R) Advanced Baseline Imager (ABI) offers superior imaging of the Earth’s environment, oceans, and weather compared to previous instruments. The ABI uses 16 different spectral channels to capture data and is the primary instrument used on the GOES-R series satellites for scanning the Western hemisphere. These channels provide four times greater spatial resolution, and more than five times the temporal coverage in near-real-time. GOES-16 scans the Midwest and east coast of the continental U.S. and GOES-18 scans the west coast and Alaska. The data captured by the ABIs are used in 30 NOAA products that are grouped into ten basic types: Clouds, Aerosols, Atmospheric Vertical Profiles, Precipitation, Winds, Land, Ocean, Radiation, and Lightning. All of the data are available from the Comprehensive Large Array Data Stewardship System (CLASS) which offers search and filter capabilities to select specific data of interest. Due to the particularly large size of the data files, data must be requested through CLASS.

title: Head Start Center Locator
author: Department of Health and Human Services (DHHS)
description: The Head Start program offers two applications for locating grant recipient services across the United States. One is the Head Start Center Locator (HSCL) which allows users to search HSCL based on the center type by state, city, or zip code. The locations for all Centers found in the requested area are displayed on an interactive map. The search results, with detailed information about each Center, is displayed below the map. Access to annual reports is also available from the More about this grantee link provided for each Center. The types of Centers include 1) Head Start Preschool, 2) Early Head Start, 3) Migrant/Seasonal Head Start, and 4) Native American/Alaska Native Head Start. The second application for finding services is the Head Start Service Location (HSSL) datasets, which is also searchable by state, city, or zip code based on the four center types shown above. The search results are less detailed and are only available for download as CSV, XML, or JSON files. The files include grant recipient name, service location and address, and the type of program provided, among other data. The information maintained in these datasets is vital to tracking the Head Start outreach and determining where and how early childhood development can be improved.

title: CDC Data Catalog
author: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC)
description: The Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC) Data Catalog is a centralized resource of health-related datasets and information that is publicly accessible. It includes a wide range of datasets collected by the CDC on public health topics such as disease prevalence, health behaviors, environmental factors, and demographic information. The catalog is searchable using free text and search filters for data type, e.g., Datasets, Maps, Charts, Forms, and/or category, e.g., Chronic Diseases (which includes numerous sub-categories), Environmental Health and Toxicology, Health Statistics, to name a few. Data from the datasets are downloadable for use in research, analysis, and decision-making to address public health issues. Metadata and documentation that describe the context, methodology, and limitations of the data are also available.

title: Center for Air, Climate, & Energy Solutions (CACES)
author: Environmental Protection Agency (EPA)
description: The Center for Air, Climate, & Energy Solutions (CACES) Empirical Model (v1) dataset provides estimated annual average, population-weighted concentrations of criteria air pollutants for states, counties, Census tracts, and Census block groups in the contiguous United States during 1979 to 2020. This dataset is based on a land use regression model that incorporates data from ground-based regulatory monitors, satellite-based observations, and land use and land cover information. This dataset covers the following criteria air pollutants: particulate matter (PM2.5 and PM10), ozone (O3), sulfur dioxide (SO2), carbon monoxide (CO), and nitrogen dioxide (NO2). The CACES Empirical Model v1 dataset is intended to support various applications in public health, risk assessment, planning, and policy. It has been used in health effects and health disparities research projects. Novel measurement and modeling approaches can be applied to understand spatial and temporal differences in human exposures and health outcomes.

title: Climate Risk and Resilience Portal (CRRPort)
author: Other
description: The Climate Risk and Resilience Portal (ClimRR) allows visualization of climate projections for the United States at a small geographic scale. The projections characterize three decadal time periods, historical (1995-2004), mid-century (2045-2054), and end-of-century (2085 – 2094)] for two carbon emission scenarios, referred to as Representative Concentration Pathway (RCP) 4.5 (emissions peak around 2040, then decline) ) and RCP 8.5 (emissions rates continue to rise). Climate projection data from three Global Climate Models that offer spatial resolutions from 100 – 200 km are combined, then a regional weather model for North America, the Weather Research and Forecasting model, is used to downscale the combined data to 12 km grid cells across the United States. The data include extreme temperatures (hot and cold), cooling and heating degree days, heat index, wind, fire weather index, and precipitation/no precipitation, which can be seen in different map layers. Data may also be viewed based on different Resilience map layers, e.g., Census Tracts: Population Age 65 and Older. Reports based on the viewing options selected are generated as PDF or CSV files. In addition, a national map for each data category shown above are available to visualize hazard layers alongside key resilience indicators, regional boundaries, and more. The insight into climate risks that ClimRR offers city and community leaders can help them prioritize vulnerable areas that need the most attention and future funding to improve resilience.

title: Consolidated Human Activity Database (CHAD)
author: Environmental Protection Agency (EPA)
description: The Consolidated Human Activity Database (CHAD) was initiated to compile pre-existing data from numerous human activity studies at the city, state and national levels. It was created to support human exposure, intake dose, and risk modeling by providing accurate information on daily human activities. CHAD was made publicly available in 2017 and includes data from studies conducted by a variety of government, private, and academic groups that date back to 1982. Data have been updated routinely throughout its development, with the most recent update occurring in 2020. The activity-data in CHAD encompasses a wide range of demographics, e.g., age, sex, employment, education, as well as records of personal activities and human behaviors that are recorded daily by study participants. These behaviors and activities are further broken down into individual hours and activity types. This allows researchers to examine specific groups within the general population to investigate how their daily activities influence exposure to chemicals in the environment. CHAD is routinely used in human health studies by EPA and other government agencies, academia, and private organizations to model human exposures and related risks.

title: EM-DAT The International Disaster Database
author: Other
description: The EM-DAT International Disaster Database contains records of worldwide natural disasters and their health and economic impacts at a country level. Disasters are commonly considered sudden, unforeseen events which cause significant damage, destruction, and human suffering that require more than local resources to manage. They necessitate external assistance at the national or international level and may include droughts, earthquakes, floods, landslides, storms, and wildfires among others. The core data in EM-DAT are obtained from numerous sources such as UN agencies, non-governmental organizations, insurance companies, research institutes, and media outlets. The University of Louvain, Centre for Research on the Epidemiology of Disasters compiles the data, maintains the database, and freely distributes the data for non-commercial use. The database contains data from 1900 to the present that are available through three data products: 1) the primary resource - living EM-DAT Public Data, updated weekly; 2) the EM-DAT archive, pre-2000 data; and 3) The EM-DAT, Humanitarian Data Exchange, Country Profiles - yearly summaries of disaster data by country.

title: Risk-Screening Environmental Indicators (RSEI) Model
author: Environmental Protection Agency (EPA)
description: The Risk-Screening Environmental Indicators (RSEI) model is a tool developed by EPA designed to help assess and evaluate the potential risks posed by environmental pollution. It incorporates publicly available EPA Toxic Release Inventory (TRI) data to estimate the potential health and environmental risks from hazardous chemicals that are released into the environment. The model provides aggregated data from more than 30 years of TRI reporting. Disaggregated results are also available in the RSEI Geographic Microdata. The RSEI model is particularly valuable for understanding the broad-scale impact of industrial pollution on environmental and public health and helping target efforts for pollution reduction and risk mitigation.

title: Clean Air Status and Trends Network (CASNET)
author: Environmental Protection Agency (EPA)
description: The Clean Air Status and Trends Network (CASTNET) is an atmospheric monitoring network that was established in response to the 1990 Clean Air Act Amendments. Air sampling sites are located throughout the U.S. and Canada to measure trends in pollutant concentrations, atmospheric deposition, and ecological effects due to changes in air pollutant emissions. The sites measure weekly ambient concentrations of pollutants that are used to monitor and assess emission reduction programs. Data from CASTNET also support assessment of the primary and secondary National Ambient Air Quality Standards for ozone, nitrogen dioxide, sulfur dioxide, and PM2.5. Raw data as well as prepackaged data sets are publicly available for download. Over the past 30 years, CASNET data have helped inform environmental policy decisions resulting in a substantive improvement in air quality.

title: EPA Ecoregions
author: Environmental Protection Agency (EPA)
description: The EPA Ecoregions (ECOR) is a classification system of ecological regions based on similarities and differences in the mosaic of biotic, abiotic, terrestrial, and aquatic components, e.g., climate, vegetation, soil type, landforms, that can affect the quality and integrity of an ecosystem. They are organized into four levels, according to the extent of detail used to define each level. The levels range from Level I, which includes 15 general regions, to Level IV, the most detailed level with >900 finely detailed regions. Maps of North American showing ecoregions Levels I, II, and III are presented with Levels II and III regions nested and displayed inside Level I. Level II has 50 regions that provide an overview of ecological patterns at a national and subcontinental level and Level III has 182 ecoregions which allows more specific ecological characteristics to be defined and enhances environmental monitoring and reporting. Maps of the continental U.S. are also presented which display ecoregions Level III and IV as well as options to view the same two ecoregions in each of the 10 EPA regions or for individual states. Level IV, that provides the finest level of detail, is useful in environmental analysis and monitoring at the local level. All of the maps offered, and their corresponding GIS files, are available for download.

title: National Emissions Inventory (NEI)
author: Environmental Protection Agency (EPA)
description: The National Emissions Inventory (NEI) reports detailed, comprehensive estimates of air emissions for criteria pollutants, criteria precursors, and hazardous air pollutants every three years (e.g., 2011, 2014, 2017, and 2020). The majority of the data are submitted by State, Local, and Tribal air agencies using the EPA Emissions Inventory System. These data may also be supplemented by EPA-estimates using data from sources such as the Toxic Release Inventory and other programs. The key elements reported in NEI include sources, pollutants, geographic, temporal, and emissions components that represent emissions estimates from four primary source categories: Point (large sources located at a fixed, stationary location, e.g., industrial facilities and electric power plants); Nonpoint (sources too small to report as point sources, e.g., residential heating, commercial combustion); Onroad (on road gasoline, diesel, and other fueled vehicles); and Nonroad (off-road mobile sources that use gasoline, diesel, and other fuels, e.g., equipment for construction, lawn and garden, and aircraft ground support). Starting in 2020, wild land fire emission estimates are being aggregated to annual and county levels and included as nonpoint source data. An NEI Exploration tool is available to help users examine and retrieve data of interest.

title: Gateway to NASA Earth Observation Data
author: National Aeronautics and Space Administration (NASA)
description: The Earth Science Data and Information Systems (ESDIS) Program provides researchers, decision makers, and the public open access to NASA’s archive of Earth science data. The data and related tools help increase the understanding of environmental changes. Data are accessed from the Gateway to NASA Earth Observation Data (GNEOD) using one of the four search or browse options: the Data Catalog, Data Tools, browse by Platform, or browse by Pathfinder Topic. The Data Catalog can be searched by keywords, data collection platform, dataset name, etc. Search results, or the entire dataset collection, can also be filtered by specific parameters such as topic, observation method, and temporal or spatial parameters, to name a few. Pathfinder Topics guide users to ESDS topic-specific datasets (6 of which are included in the CHORDS Catalog). The 10 topics include: Atmosphere, Land Surface, Biosphere, Ocean, Climate Indicators, Solid Earth, Cryosphere Sun-Earth Interactions, Human Dimensions, and Terrestrial Hydrosphere. Data Tools provide access to a variety of tools for data discovery and visualization. Three tools are featured: 1) Worldview – allows browsing global imagery and downloading underlying data; 2) Vertex - a graphical search interface of synthetic aperture radar land use/cover data; and 3) Giovanni - Geospatial Interactive Online Visualization ANd aNalysis Infrastructure.

title: Meteorological Assimilation Data Ingest System (MADIS)
author: National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration (NOAA)
description: NOAA’s Meteorological Assimilation Data Ingest System (MADIS) is a comprehensive, centralized data integration system developed to collect, quality-control, and distribute real-time observational data from a wide range of in weather forecasting, climate research, and emergency response sources. Data from both NOAA and non-NOAA sources, such as surface weather stations, aircraft, radar, satellite, and ocean buoys, are decoded and then all of the observational data are encoded into a common format with uniform observation units and time stamps. Rigorous automated quality checks on all incoming data identify and flag anomalies or errors to allow users to inspect these data and decide whether to exclude them from analyses. As of March 2023, MADIS became fully operational under the management of the National Weather Service (NWS). Access to real-time and archived observational data plus real-time surface analyses grids is available via the online Data Application MADIS has played a key role in improving short-term weather forecasts, enhancing situational awareness during severe weather events, supporting climate monitoring, and aiding in transportation and public safety operations across the United States.

title: National Arbovirus Surveillance System (ArboNET)
author: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC)
description: ArboNET is a national surveillance system managed by the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC) that tracks arboviral diseases (arthropod-borne viruses) in the United States. It monitors diseases transmitted by mosquitos, ticks, and other vectors. ArboNET collects data on human cases, non-human cases (birds, horses, and mosquitoes), and environmental factors. Reports come from state and local health departments, which track cases in collaboration with the CDC. The data helps inform public health responses, mosquito control efforts, and research.

title: National Arbovirus Surveillance System (ArboNET)
author: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC)
description: ArboNET is a national surveillance system managed by the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC) that tracks arboviral diseases (arthropod-borne viruses) in the United States. It monitors diseases transmitted by mosquitos, ticks, and other vectors. ArboNET collects data on human cases, non-human cases (birds, horses, and mosquitoes), and environmental factors. Reports come from state and local health departments, which track cases in collaboration with the CDC. The data helps inform public health responses, mosquito control efforts, and research.

title: National Digital Forecast Database (NDFD)
author: National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration (NOAA)
description: The National Digital Forecast Database (NDFD) is a suite of gridded digital forecasts of basic weather elements (e.g., cloud cover, maximum temperature) that is generated by the National Weather Service (NWS) in collaboration with regional NWS Weather Forecast Offices (WFOs) and the National Centers for Environmental Prediction (NCEP). Data obtained from WFOs and NCEP are combined to create the NDFD digital forecasts. The gridded data are presented as map displays for weekly forecast periods with links at each location to graphical representations of the data along with a forecast discussion. Access to download data as General Regularly-distributed Information in Binary (GRIB) formatted files is also available using ftp and https transfer protocols or the AWS S3 Explorer (https://noaa-ndfd-pds.s3.amazonaws.com/index.html).

title: National Hydrography Dataset Plus Version 2.1 (NHDplusV2)
author: Environmental Protection Agency (EPA)
description: The National Hydrography Dataset Plus Version 2.1 (NHDplusV2) provides a national geospatial surface-water framework that is used by geospatial analysts and modelers to support their water resource applications. It is an enhanced version of NHDplusV1 produced by the U.S. EPA Office of Water in partnership with the U.S. Geological Survey. NHDplusV2 is an integrated suite of application-ready geospatial data products that integrates detailed hydrologic features such as streams, rivers, lakes, reservoirs, and watersheds with attributes related to flow direction, flow accumulation, and watershed boundaries. It utilizes and extends the best features of the National Hydrography Dataset (NHD), the National Elevation Dataset (NED), and the National Watershed Boundary Dataset (WBD). The spatial resolution for NHD and NED have been improved to 1:100k and NED 30m, respectively. NHDplusV2 integrates additional attributes such as elevation, land use, and climate to enhance hydrological modeling, watershed analysis, flood forecasting, and water resource management. Improvements to catchment characteristics, headwater node areas, and cumulative drainage characteristics have been implemented along with advanced methods for estimating monthly and annual river and stream flow volumes and velocities. NHDplus2 is a vital resource for government agencies, decision makers, and researchers.

title: NOAA Operational Model Archive and Distribution System (NOMADS)
author: National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration (NOAA)
description: The NOAA Operational Model Archive and Distribution System (NOMADS) is a web-based service provided by NOAA for distributing real-time and archived Numerical Weather Prediction model data. It is a key resource for researchers, forecasters, and developers who need access to high-quality weather model outputs. NOMADS provides access to a wide range of model data including: GFS (Global Forecast System), NAM (North American Mesoscale), RAP/HRRR (Rapid Refresh, High-Resolution Rapid Refresh), GEFS (Global Ensemble Forecast System), CMC (Canadian Meteorological Centre models) – select models, Wave models, ocean, and climate forecast data.

title: Office of Satellite and Products Operations Atmosphere Product (OSOP-AP)
author: National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration (NOAA)
description: The Office of Satellite and Products Operations, Atmosphere Products (OSPO-AP), is a collection of over 20 separate atmospheric data products that are grouped into seven categories: Aerosol, Clouds, Ozone/Atmospheric Chemistry, Precipitation, Soundings, Volcanic Ash, and Winds. Each category includes individual data products and measurements derived from NOAA's advanced satellite systems. These data are essential for monitoring and understanding atmospheric changes and how they affect the Earth’s environment. Many of the key Atmosphere products use near-real time, satellite imagery for high-resolution visual representations of cloud cover, storms, and other atmospheric features recorded for the current month. The satellites include geostationary operational satellites (GOES), polar-orbiting environmental satellites (POES), and Joint Polar Satellite Systems (JPSS). The data in the Atmosphere products are used by meteorologists, researchers, and decision-makers in support of their efforts to understand and predict weather patterns. The history of NOAA’s satellite program and instrumentation, starting in 1970, is also available at https://www.nesdis.noaa.gov/our-satellites/related-information/history-of-noaa-satellites.

title: Office of Satellite and Products Operations Land Product (OSPO-LP)
author: National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration (NOAA)
description: The Office of Satellite and Products Operations, Land Products (OSPO-LP), is a collection of 20 separate data products that are subset into four categories: Fire & Smoke, Snow & Ice, Surface & Hydrology and Vegetation. The individual data products and measurements are derived from NOAA's advanced global satellite systems and provide important information about the earth's land surface that is useful for environmental monitoring, land management, agriculture, and urban planning. High-resolution visual representations of land features, vegetation cover, and land use patterns enable monitoring land surface changes, such as deforestation, urban expansion, and agricultural activities. This information is critical for supporting climate modeling and environmental impact assessments, land management decisions, and assessing natural resource sustainability.

title: Office of Satellite and Products Operations Ocean Products
author: National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration (NOAA)
description: NOAA's Office of Satellite and Product Operations (OSPO) provides a variety of products, including Ocean Products (OP) derived from satellite observations. The OP products support weather forecasting, climate monitoring, and marine operations. Some key ocean products include: 1. Sea Surface Temperature (SST); global and regional SST measurements for climate studies, weather prediction, and marine navigation. 2. Ocean Color & Chlorophyll; monitors ocean biological activity, including chlorophyll concentration, used for assessing phytoplankton levels and ecosystem health. 3. Sea Surface Height & Ocean Circulation; uses altimetry data to track sea level changes, ocean currents, and eddies. 4. Ocean Winds; provides wind speed and direction over the ocean, crucial for maritime navigation and weather forecasting. 5. Coral Reef Watch; tracks sea surface temperature anomalies to predict coral bleaching events. 6. Harmful Algal Blooms (HAB); detects and identifies potential HAB that can impact marine life and human health. 7. Sea Ice Monitoring; observes ice concentration, extent, and movement, supporting navigation in polar regions and climate research. These products rely on satellite sensors such as VIIRS and MODIS for data collection.

title: OneStop Data Search Platform (OneStop)
author: National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration (NOAA)
description: OneStop is an open-sourced metadata storage, management, and discovery system designed to streamline access to a broad range of environmental data collected by NOAA. The search platform has a user-friendly interface to search, discover, and access datasets that include weather, climate, oceanography, and other critical environmental science, all in one location, and uses Boolean logic and attribute filters to refine search results. The platform is designed to help improve the relevance of dataset searches, and advance both collection-level metadata management and granule-level metadata systems to handle the wide variety and scale of NOAA's data. One key objective for OneStop is to help facilitate informed decision-making and promote greater collaboration within the scientific and regulatory communities.

title: Regional Hourly ABI and VIIRS Emissions (RAVE)
author: National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration (NOAA)
description: The Regional ABI and VIIRS Emissions (RAVE) Version 2 is an advanced biomass-burning emissions inventory developed to enhance air quality forecasting across North America. This system integrates high-temporal-resolution data from the Advanced Baseline Imager (ABI) on NOAA's GOES satellites with fine-spatial-resolution data from the Visible Infrared Imaging Radiometer Suite (VIIRS) on JPSS satellites to provide hourly emissions estimates at a spatial resolution of approximately 3 km. The emissions data include various pollutants such as carbon dioxide (CO2), carbon monoxide (CO), fine particulate matter (PM2.5), sulfur dioxide (SO2, organic carbon (OC), black carbon (BC), nitrogen oxides (NOx), ammonia (NH3), methane (CH4), and volatile organic compounds (VOCs). RAVE is one of six data products available in NOAA's Fire and Smoke collection found under the Office of Satellite and Product Operations Land Products.

title: STAR - Global Vegetation Health Products
author: National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration (NOAA)
description: ​The Center for Satellite Applications and Research (STAR), part of NOAA's National Environmental Satellite, Data, and Information Service, offers Global Vegetation Health Products that monitor and assess vegetation conditions worldwide. These products utilize data from instruments like the Advanced Very High-Resolution Radiometer and the Visible Infrared Imaging Radiometer Suite to generate indices that include the Vegetation Health Index, Vegetation Condition Index, and Temperature Condition Index. These indices serve as proxies for factors like moisture and thermal conditions, aiding in applications such as drought monitoring, crop assessment, and tracking ecological changes

title: OpenLandMap
author: Other
description: OpenLandMap (OLM) is an Open Land Data service hosted by OpenGeoHub that provides access to datasets and spatial layers that cover global land mass. OLM strives to integrate, harmonize, and process the wide range of publicly available, global legacy land data (land cover, vegetation, soil, climate, terrain data and similar) using state-of-the-art Machine Learning algorithms. The process uses a federated approach that involves researchers and programmers from a variety of organizations and institutions. Efforts are made to ensure data are Analysis-Ready and Cloud-Optimized such that they are of the highest quality, fully documented, and optimized for web-services and advanced analysis. The resulting datasets and layers are presented as a stack of gridded environmental layers that are organized around themes, such as Land Cover and Land Use, Physical Infrastructure, Geology and Soils, and Population Distribution. They are accessed from the OML portal and, unless otherwise specified, are open data, licensed under the Open Data Commons Open Database License (ODbL) and/or Creative Commons Attribution-ShareAlike 4.0 International license (CC BY-SA) by the OpenGeoHub Foundation. This allows users to copy, distribute, transmit and adapt data for use provided they credit OpenLandMap.org and its contributors.

title: Parameter-elevation Regressions on Independent Slopes Model (PRISM) Spatial Climate Datasets for the Conterminous United States, Version D2
author: Other
description: Parameter-elevation Regressions on Independent Slopes Model (PRISM) contains daily gridded climate observation data from a wide range of ground-based monitoring networks. The data are displayed in the Spatial Climate Datasets for the Conterminous United States, Daily Time Series, using spatial interpolation and simulation methods. The data encompass seven primary climate elements: precipitation, minimum temperature, maximum temperature, mean dew point, minimum vapor pressure deficit, maximum vapor pressure deficit, and total shortwave solar radiation on a horizontal surface. The cover the period from 1895 to present and reveal long- and short-term climate patterns.

title: Safe Drinking Water Information System
author: Environmental Protection Agency (EPA)
description: The Safe Drinking Water Information System (SDWIS) is a database maintained by the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) that contains information about public drinking water systems. It tracks states' compliance with the Safe Drinking Water Act (SDWA) and records violations pertaining to water quality and reporting requirements. The database can be queried using either basic or advanced search options based on report variables that include Public Water System (PWS) ID, PWS Name, PWS Type (e.g., Community Water System), Primary Source, Counties Served, Cities Served, Population, Served Count, Number of Facilities, Number of Violations, and Number of Site Visits. Links within the search results table are available to see more details from SDWIS reports.

title: Tick data
author: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC)
description: The CDC offers a comprehensive collection of data and resources on tickborne diseases in the United States. These resources are designed to assist healthcare professionals, researchers, and the public in understanding the prevalence, distribution, and impact of diseases transmitted by ticks. In this resource they provide Tickborne Disease Surveillance Data and comprehensive Summary, Tick Bite Data Tracker, and Geographic Distribution of Tickborne Disease through the Disease Surveillance Data.

title: USGS Products
author: United States Geological Survey (USGS)
