description: The USGS Products are an extensive collection of publicly available resources that encompass a broad range of geological topics, including natural disaster that threaten human lives and health, natural resources, and the health/changes in earth’s ecosystems and environment, including the impact on land-use change. The information provided in the products supports decision-making on environmental, resource, and public safety issues. Some products of interest include: 1) Data products that are provided as data releases from modeling and analysis of specific datasets, or as real-time data that are collected periodically and/or from continuous monitoring in the field. Real-time data contain information about Earthquakes, Floods, Wildfires, Water conditions, Volcanoes, Landslides, and Drought; 2) Software for data modeling and analysis. Some applications include samples of processed data; 3) Web Tools for data access, analysis, and visualizations, along with access to digital data repositories, and interactive maps; and 4) Maps that present geologic, topographic, volcanic, and other related information in interactive maps. Each data product offers text search capabilities and options to filter results by data type, topic, and year.

title: Extreme Heat Data Pathfinder (Heat-Pathfinder)
author: National Aeronautics and Space Administration (NASA)
description: The Heat Data Pathfinder (Heat-Pathfinder) is one of several Data Pathfinders NASA designed to help guide users through the process of selecting and using application-specific datasets from NASA’s Earth Science data collections. It provides access to reliable, global climate data records for use in assessing environmental change. Long-term data records from monitoring heat waves are used to differentiate abnormalities from normal climate conditions. The records contain robust, sustainable, and scientifically sound data of temperature, humidity, and other environmental metrics that have been collected over decades and includes a joint NOAA/NASA data set of sea surface temperatures for studying the relationship between abnormal heat events and sea surface temperatures.

title: InciWeb
author: United States Forestry Service (USFS)
description: InciWeb is an interagency, all-risk, wildland fire incident, information management system that is supported by the United States Forestry Service (USFS) and many other federal agencies. It provides the public and Public Affairs community a single source of real-time incidents, fire behavior, fuel models, and other related information from a standardized reporting tool. Numerous support systems automate the delivery of incident information to remote sources, which ensures that the information regarding active incidents is consistent, and the delivery is timely. Links to additional resources for reports and statistics, mapping tools, and national wildland fire agencies are also available.

title: Industrial Emissions Data Pathfinder (IE-Pathfinder)
author: National Aeronautics and Space Administration (NASA)
description: The Industrial Emissions (IE) Pathfinder is one of several Data Pathfinders NASA designed to help guide users through the process of selecting and using application-specific datasets from NASA’s Earth science data collections. The EI Pathfinder uses datasets acquired through collaboration with other federal entities and international space organizations from satellite, airborne, and ground-based instruments that monitor emissions. Links to the different data sources as well as visualization tools and other resources are also available.

title: gridMET
author: Other
description: The gridMET dataset provides near real-time daily surface meteorological information from 1979 to the present. The dataset covers the contiguous United States and southern British Columbia, Canada, with spatial resolution of ~4 km (1/24th degree). Data is updated daily with the most recent available data for the previous day. The dataset provides a range of surface meteorological variables, such as temperature, humidity, and wind speed, that are used as inputs for ecological models. Additionally, gridMET provides several variables, such as the burning index and energy release component, that are used by fire management agencies to assess wildfire potential. The meteorology dataset is produced using climatically aided interpolation with a combination of a higher spatial resolution dataset (PRISM) and a long-term higher temporal resolution dataset (NLDAS-2)

title: TerraClimate
author: Other
description: TerraClimate provides monthly climate and climatic water balance data during the time period from 1958 to 2024. The dataset covers global land surfaces with spatial resolution of ~4 km (1/24th degree). Variables include maximum temperature, minimum temperature, precipitation, wind speed, runoff, and soil moisture, among others. TerraClimate data can be used in various ecological and hydrological applications, such as species distribution modeling, environment impact analyses, and wildfire activity pattern studies. The climate data is produced using climatically aided interpolation with a combination of a higher spatial resolution dataset (WorldClim) and two long-term higher temporal resolution datasets (CRU Ts4.0 and JRA-55). The climatic water balance data is produced using a water balance model with extractable soil water storage capacity data.

title: Landscape Fire and Resource Management Planning Tools (LANDFIRE)
author: United States Department of Agriculture (USDA)
description: The Landscape Fire and Resource Management Planning Tools (LANDFIRE) program is a federally sponsored, collaborative initiative that includes a number of supporting partners, e.g., The Nature Conservancy and the U.S. Geological Survey. LANDFIRE is designed to provide comprehensive, up-to-date, consistent geospatial data and tools for use in land management and planning. It integrates data to create detailed maps and datasets that cover various aspects of the landscape, including vegetation, wildland fuel, fire behavior, and ecological conditions throughout the U.S. It is intended for use at a landscape-scale to support strategic vegetation, fire, and fuels management planning for evaluating management alternatives across boundaries. LANDFIRE is used by fire and natural resource managers, planners, policymakers, and researchers to support informed decision-making but is also available to the public.

title: National Environmental Public Health Tracking Network (NEPHTN)
author: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC)
description: The National Environmental Health Public Health Tracking Network (NEPHTN) collects, aggregates, analyzes, and integrates a wealth of health and environmental data sets gathered from the national, state, and city level. The program provides comprehensive visualization tools, an API to access and contribute data, access to data at the census tract and sub-county level, and multiple indicators of environmental health. Data, presented as derived measures, are organized by indicator and content area and are searchable on the Data Explorer tool. In addition, several topic-specific “Powered by Tracking” dashboards are available. These interactive data tools provide a more complete, detailed picture of important environmental health topics all in one place.

title: National Interagency Fire Center (NIFC)
author: United States Forestry Service (USFS)
description: The National Interagency Fire Center (NIFC) is responsible for coordinating interagency efforts to combat wildfires and implement strategies for fire suppression and prevention in the U.S. It collects and stores a wide range of wildfire-related data (Parker et al. 2021) including information on burned areas, fire counts (Balch et al., 2020; Son. et al., 2022), and other related spatial data. These data along with coordination efforts help inform strategies for fire suppression, prevention, and mitigation. Additional resources, such as WFIGS, MODIS, InciWeb, and others, are also available from NIFC Open Data Site that offer more data and information to help manage and reduce the impact of wildfires on community welfare.

title: NOAA Fire Weather (FireWeather)
author: National Weather Service (NWS)
description: Fire Weather is operated by the National Weather Service and plays a crucial role in assessing and predicting fire weather conditions. It is dedicated to providing specialized weather support to wildland fire management agencies across the U.S. to aid firefighting efforts in mitigating risks, enhancing safety, and improving operational effectiveness. Advanced weather modeling techniques and real-time data analysis are used to predict fire behavior, monitor fire progression, and inform critical decision-making processes during wildfire events. The service also relies on scientific research and data analysis to understand the impact of environmental changes on fire activity.

title: Open Air Quality (OpenAQ)
author: Other
description: Open Air Quality (OpenAQ) is the largest open-source air quality data platform in the world. Real-time and historical data on a wide range of air pollutants are maintained in a comprehensive data repository that includes data for PM2.5 and PM10, Ozone, NO2, SO2, CO, and VOCs. Disparate ground-level ambient air data from reference-grade government monitors and air sensors across the globe are aggregated and harmonized on the data platform. The data are universally accessible to aid in understanding air pollution for making corrective action to improve air quality and air inequity.

title: Purple Air
author: PurpleAir
description: PurpleAir collects measurements of PM2.5 concentrations from a public network of sensors that continuously monitor air quality. Real-time data are transmitted to PurpleAir servers where they are aggregated and made publicly available. Monitors are distributed worldwide, both indoors and outdoors, which gives a comprehensive view of regional air quality. There are almost 12,000, 24-hour averaged, PM2.5 measurements collected from collocated sensors and Federal Reference Method (FRM) or Federal Equivalent Method (FEM) PM2.5 measurements in the U.S. alone (Barkjohn et al., 2021). Studies that have evaluated performance, challenges, and data integration have indicated that, although the system has application for longitudinal ambient PM2.5 measurement, the accuracy of the data may be influenced by the location of the sensors and their installation.

title: RAP Chem Model Fields - Experimental (RAP-Chem)
author: National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration (NOAA)
description: Rapid Refresh (RAP) is a weather prediction system comprised primarily of a numerical forecast model and analysis/assimilation system to initialize that model, which runs every hour. It covers North America and was developed for users needing frequently updated short-range weather forecasts. The RAP Chem Model Fields (RAP-Chem) is an experimental component of Rapid Refresh Chem Plots and includes a number of forecast data, such as wind, temperature, precipitation, fire, air quality, and surface chemicals which are displayed across a static map of North America. RAP is complemented by the higher-resolution 3 km High-Resolution Rapid Refresh model, which is also updated hourly but covers a smaller geographic domain.

title: Smoke Sense
author: Environmental Protection Agency (EPA)
description: Smoke Sense is a program designed to engage citizen participation to raise awareness about the health effects of wildfire smoke, empower individuals to take protective actions, and improve the understanding of how wildfire smoke affects communities. Through the use of a mobile application, participants report their experiences and symptoms related to wildfire smoke exposure, providing valuable data for researchers and public health officials. The program also serves as an educational tool and resource to increase awareness and encourage people to take steps to protect their health from wildfire smoke.

title: Time Series of Potential U.S. Wildland Fire Smoke Exposures (WFSmokeExp)
author: California Department of Public Health
description: The Time Series of Potential US Wildland Fire Smoke Exposures (WFSmokeExp) data represent a spatial and temporal intersection of U.S. population data with wildfire smoke data from 2010 – 2019. Data from NOAA’s Hazard Mapping System’s Smoke Product are combined with data from the U.S. Census Block Group Population Centers to estimate a potential exposure using light, medium, or heavy categories. The result is a table of locations and daily smoke observations with each row displaying data for a single block group and day. The data are stored in csv format in the Harvard Dataverse Repository, a free data repository, and are useful in helping to understand the burden of wildfire smoke on communities across the U.S. The data set also encourages more research on impacts of wildfire smoke on the health of U.S. Populations.

title: U.S. Fire Administration (USFA)
author: Federal Emergency Management Agency (FEMA)
description: The United States Fire Administration (USFA) provides fire incidence data through their National Fire Incidence Reporting System (NFIRS). Data are received quarterly from participating fire departments across the continental U.S. and compiled annually into Public Data Releases (PDRs), which are available for download from OpenFEMA for the years 1980-2022. The PDR is a relational database of 20 tables that are tied together by a 2-, 5- or 6-variable key. The data have been used by stakeholders to prepare for, prevent, mitigate, and respond to fire incidents and have helped reduce the numbers of fire deaths thus making communities and residents across the nation safer. Data analysis tools and additional data sets, such as Wildfires, Fire Loss in the United States, and national and state fire statistics are also available.

title: Wildfires Data Pathfinder (WF-Pathfinder)
author: National Aeronautics and Space Administration (NASA)
description: The Wildfire Data Pathfinder is one of several Data Pathfinders NASA designed to help guide users through the process of selecting and using application-specific datasets from NASA’s Earth science data collections. NASA collaborates with other federal entities and international space organizations to provide Wildfire information and imagery using satellite, airborne, and terrestrial systems. The resources utilized have significantly advanced the detection and monitoring of wildfires. Links to the different data sources as well as visualization tools and other resources are also available.

title: US National Aeronautics and Space Administration (NASA) Global Modeling and Assimilation Office (GMAO) Modern-Era Retrospective analysis for Research and Applications Version 2 (MERRA-2) tavgM_2d_chm_Nx: 2-dimensional, Monthly mean, Time-Averaged, Single-Level, Assimilation, Carbon Monoxide and Ozone Diagnostics (M2TMNXCHM), Version 5.12.4
author: National Aeronautics and Space Administration (NASA)
description: A monthly time-averaged 2-dimensional data collection of atmospheric carbon monoxide and ozone parameters is presented in the Modern-Era Retrospective analysis for Research and Applications version 2 (MERRA-2). It provides global model and data assimilation results that incorporate satellite-based observations to predict monthly carbon monoxide and ozone parameters, such as properties of carbon monoxide (column burden, emission, chemical production, and surface concentration), and total column ozone.

title: Hazard Mapping System (HMS) Smoke Product
author: National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration (NOAA)
description: The National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration (NOAA) Hazard Mapping System (HMS) Smoke Product provides the locations of smoke plumes associated with active fires in North America in near real-time. Smoke plume locations are identified through expert visual analysis of satellite-based imagery during daylight. Additionally, smoke plumes are classified by their density as heavy, medium, or light. The HMS smoke plume data is available on a daily basis from 2003 to the present.

title: Fioletov et al. (2010) Ultraviolet (UV) Exposure Levels in North America
author: Other
description: The Fioletov et al. (2010) Ultraviolet (UV) Exposure Levels in North America dataset presents estimated hourly and monthly long-term average ultraviolet (UV) irradiance as gridded data for the United States and Canada. It is derived from ground-based monitor and satellite-based observation data, and general exposure factors. Data are presented as a vitamin D weighted UV dose.

title: American Community Survey (ACS)
author: United States Census Bureau (US Census)
description: The American Community Survey (ACS) is a large-scale ongoing community survey conducted by the U.S. Census that provides yearly updated, detailed data on population, economics, demographics, housing, and other related information.Various guides, tutorials, and tools to support use of the data are also available at https://www.census.gov/programs-surveys/acs/guidance.html for guidance.

title: Geospatial Research, Analysis and Services Program, CDC/ATSDR Social Vulnerability Index (GRASP-CDC/ATSDR-SVI)
author: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC)
description: The Geospatial Research, Analysis and Services Program, CDC/ATSDR Social Vulnerability Index (GRASP-CDC/ATSDR -SVI) database incorporates various socioeconomic factors, such as poverty, education, housing, and minority status, to identify areas that may be disproportionately affected by environmental and other disasters. By analyzing these factors, the SVI helps public health officials and emergency planners prioritize resources and interventions to support vulnerable communities and reduce disparities in disaster preparedness, response, and recovery efforts. It covers 16 U.S. census variables and helps local officials identify communities that most likely need support before, during, and after public health emergencies.

title: Community Resilience Estimates (CRE)
author: United States Census Bureau (US Census)
description: The Community Resilience Estimates (CRE) tracks the social vulnerability of every single neighborhood in the U.S. to the impact of local disasters. It measures the capacity of individuals and households to absorb, endure, and recover from the health, social, and economic impacts of a disaster such as a hurricane or pandemic. Small area resilience estimates are used to identify communities where resources and information may effectively mitigate the impact of disasters. These estimates can help facilitate disaster preparedness and aid stakeholders and public health officials model differential impacts to develop plans to reduce a disaster’s potential effects.

title: Environmental Justice Index (EJI)
author: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC)
description: The Environmental Justice Index (EJI) is a high-level mapping and screen tool that provides data on the health impacts of different measures of environmental justice across the U.S. at the census tract level based on data from several federal resources. It includes an EJI ranking at the community level (census tract) based on environmental burden, social vulnerability, and health vulnerability.

title: Environmental Justice Screening and Mapping Tool (EJScreen)
author: Environmental Protection Agency (EPA)
description: The Environmental Justice Screening and Mapping Tool (EJScreen) is a U.S. EPA environmental justice mapping and screening tool. It provides a nationally consistent dataset and approach for combining environmental and demographic socioeconomic indicators to highlight places that may have higher environmental burdens and vulnerable populations. Users choose a geographic area and the tool then provides demographic socioeconomic and environmental information for that area. All of the indicators are publicly-available. EJScreen simply provides a way to display the information and a method for combining environmental and demographic indicators into EJ indexes.

title: Blended Global Biomass Burning Emissions Product - Extended V.3 (GBBEPxV3)
author: National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration (NOAA)
description: The Blended Global Biomass Burning Emissions Product – Extended, version 3 (GBBEPx V3) system uses advanced algorithms to combine satellite-based observation data from three biomass burning emissions datasets to produce blended global daily biomass burning emission estimates. The datasets include MODIS Quick Fire Emission Dataset (QFED), VIIRS (NPP and JPSS-1) fire emissions, and Global Biomass Burning Emission Product from Geostationary satellites. Emission estimates are calculated for atmospheric pollutants released during wildfires and agricultural burnings. These pollutants include PM2.5, ammonia (NH3), black carbon (BC), carbon dioxide (CO2), carbon monoxide (CO), nitrogen dioxide (NO2), organic carbon (OC), and sulfur dioxide (SO2). The data, displayed on a global map at 0.1 x 0.1 degree spatial resolution, cover the current 30-day period. Additional system output includes fire detection records, spatial pattern of PM2.5 emissions, and continental statistics for PM2.5.

title: Hazard Mapping System Fire and Smoke Product (HMS)
author: National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration (NOAA)
description: The Hazard Mapping System (HMS) Fire and Smoke Product is a valuable tool for monitoring and predicting the behavior of fires and smoke. The continental United States is monitored 24 hours a day, year round by polar and geostationary satellites that provide near real-time data on fire location, intensity, and extent as well as smoke analysis results based on visual classification of plumes. The system displays active fire detections and smoke information for each 24 hour period, starting with the earliest observations of the day, on a fresh map of North America. This information is essential for assessing the impacts of fires on air quality, public health, and the environment.

title: Healthcare Cost and Utilization Project (HCUP)
author: Agency for Healthcare Research and Quality (AHRQ)
description: The Healthcare Cost and Utilization Project (HCUP) is a collection of healthcare databases, and related software tools and products, developed through a Federal-State-Industry partnership. The databases are a national information resource of encounter-level healthcare data produced from the efforts of State data organizations, hospital associations, private data organizations, and the Federal government. They enable research on a broad range of health policy issues, including cost and quality of health services, medical practice patterns, access to healthcare programs, and outcomes of treatments at the national, State, and local market levels.

title: Kaiser Permanente Research Bank (KPRB)
author: Kaiser Foundation Health Plans
description: Kaiser Permanente Research Bank (KPRB) is a core research resource that provides data and biospecimens to enable discoveries that contribute to health knowledge, including the prevention, diagnosis, management and treatment of disease. The KPRB data and biospecimens come from members recruited by the KPRB, as well as earlier KP biobanked collections dating back to the 1990s. The data are standardized and harmonized across regions using the KP/CESR Virtual Data Warehouse. Genomic data are also available for 103K participants who were genotyped on an Axiom Array in 2010 using four custom microarrays for each major racial and/or ethnicity group represented in the group. Additional genotyping of participants is continuing and demonstrates KP’s commitment to advancing genetic research and its potential impact on public health (Gaziano et al., 2016).

title: Moderate Resolution Imaging Spectroradiometer (MODIS)
author: National Aeronautics and Space Administration (NASA)
description: The Moderate Resolution Imaging Spectroradiometer (MODIS) resource provides moderate resolution imaging data of the earth's total surface every two days. Data are collected by spectroradiometers onboard two NASA satellites that detect electromagnetic energy across 36 spectral bands ranging from 0.4 to 14.4 micrometers which allows many of the earth’s rapid biological and meteorological changes to be captured. The processed data are made available through numerous data products for studies of earth’s evolving air and land features. They are also distributed and used by multiple partners that specialize in different data products (see the MODIS web site for a list of partner distribution sites, plus the NASA EarthData portal and NASA Data Pathfinder web sites). MODIS plays a vital role in the development of validated, global, interactive earth system models able to predict global change accurately enough to assist policy makers with decisions concerning environmental protection.

title: National death index (NDI)
author: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC)
description: The National Death Index (NDI) was developed to be a centralized database of death record information compiled from state vital statistics offices to aid epidemiologists and other health and medical investigators with their mortality ascertainment activities. It provides the public health and medical research community access to mortality follow-up information on their study participants, without having to go from jurisdiction to jurisdiction.

title: National Health and Nutrition Examination Survey (NHANSE)
author: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC)
description: The National Health and Nutrition Examination Survey (NHANES) is a comprehensive survey that collects data on the health and nutritional status on adults and children from representative samplings of the U.S. population. Data collected from questions about demographic, socioeconomic, dietary, and health-related factors are used to determine the prevalence of major diseases and risk factors for diseases. The data are available online from the NHANES Interactive Data Visualization tool and have been a valuable resource for researchers and policymakers in understanding the health and nutritional status of the U.S. population and have contributed to the development of guidelines and policies.

title: PLACES
author: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC)
description: PLACES offers local data for better health that covers the entire U.S. It provides health data for small areas across the country using the small area (census tract, zipcode) estimation method based on the Behavioral Risk Factor Surveillance Systems and American Community Survey. PLACES reports county, place, census tract, and zipcode data from estimates of health outcomes, preventive service use, health risk behaviors, disabilities, and health status. It is an expansion of the CDC and Robert Wood Johnson Foundation, 500 Cities Project that began in 2015.

title: Social Determinants of Health (SDOH)
author: Agency for Healthcare Research and Quality (AHRQ)
description: The AHRQ Social Determinants of Health (SDOH) database provides easy to use, linkable, SDOH-focused data to use in  patient-centered outcome research, inform approaches to address emerging health issues, and  contribute to improved health outcomes. The database contains more than 600 measures at the county scale (more than 300 at the census tract scale) drawn from 44 data sources in 5 domains (social context, economic context, education, physical infrastructure, and healthcare context). It includes annual data files and codebooks at three levels of geography (county, ZIP Code, and census tract), as well as a documentation file.

title: Washington State All Payer Claims Database (WA-APCD)
author: Washington State Health Care Authority
description: The Washington State All Payer Claims Database (WA-APCD) makes health care claims data available to the public for reporting, analytics, and health care decisions in response to state legislation requirements. The data help identify best practices and good value to assist patients, providers, and hospitals make informed choices, while encouraging competition in cost and quality of care. The WA-APCD provides information on costs of over 100 procedures and treatments, quality ratings for providers and facilities, and information on how to request customized data sets and reports. Data are provided for a fee, based on user specifications.

title: Wide-ranging Online Data for Epidemiologic Research (WONDER)
author: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC)
description: The Wide-ranging Online Data for Epidemiologic Research (WONDER) provides a website and query tools for accessing a wide range of public health data sets gathered by the Center for Disease Conrol (CDC). The data sets cover a  range of topics, such as births, deaths, cancer diagnoses, tuberculosis cases, vaccinations, environmental exposures, population estimates, and more. Data is sourced from various government agencies and collated by the CDC.

title: National Occupational Mortality Surveillance
author: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC)
description: The National Occupational Mortality Surveillance (NOMS) program is coordinated by the CDC, National Institute for Occupational Safety and Health (NIOSH). NOMS is a surveillance system that monitors and analyzes mortality data by occupation and industry code included on death certificates to identify work-related causes of death. This helps detect patterns that may be indicative of health risks associated with certain jobs. NOMS has helped reveal: 1) High rates of mesothelioma in construction and shipyard workers (linked to asbestos exposure); 2) Increased suicide rates in certain occupations (like farming and construction); and 3) Patterns of lung disease among coal miners and other industrial workers. Data are also accessible from the National Vital Statistics System.

title: US National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration (NOAA) National Centers for Environmental Prediction (NCEP) North American Regional Reanalysis (NARR) model
author: National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration (NOAA)
description: The North American Regional Reanalysis (NARR) model is a high-resolution atmospheric and land-surface product of the National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration (NOAA), National Centers for Environmental Prediction. It offers a long-term, consistent, climate dataset that combines model output with high-resolution observational data to provide a detailed picture of the North American climate system. It incorporates a wide range of atmospheric, oceanic, and land surface observations using advanced data assimilation techniques.

title: US Federal Emergency Management Agency (FEMA) National Risk Index (NRI) for Natural Hazards, Version 1.19.0 (March 2023 Release)
author: Federal Emergency Management Agency (FEMA)
description: The National Risk Index (NRI) for Natural Hazards dataset helps identify United States communities most at risk to 18 types of natural hazards including, riverine flood, coastal flood, hurricane, and wildfire. Composite risk scores (percentiles), based on data from various sources of Expected Annual Loss, after adjusting for Social Vulnerability and Community Resilience, represent community baseline risk. Scores are reviewed by federal, state, and local government, academia, and private industry subject matter experts, and are available at the census tract and county spatial scale from interactive maps and user generated reports.

title: NOAA GOES-R Series Advanced Baseline Imager (ABI) Level 2 Fire/Hot Spot Characterization (FDC)
author: National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration (NOAA)
description: The GOES-R Series Advanced Baseline Imager (ABI) Level 2, Fire/Hot Spot Characterization product (GOES-R_FDC) uses data collected by the NOAA, GOES-16 and GOES-17 satellites for the Western Hemisphere, which are processed by the GOES-R Algorithm Working Group in preparation for use by GOES-R_FDC. Data are displayed using four images. One is a fire mask that shows fire location, and the other three are pixel values that estimate fire size, temperature, and radiative power. The output is a kind of pixel level metadata that is useful in real-time and offline model integration and assessment. The pixel values in the fire mask image are also used to flag fire category and diagnostic information associated. The categories include Good quality fire pixel; Saturated fire pixel; Cloud contaminated fire pixel; High probability fire pixel; Medium probability fire pixel; Low probability fire pixel. The words “temporally filtered” precede the category name when the pixels are in close proximity for both space and time. The product image is displayed at 2 km resolution.

title: North Carolina Community Mapping System (NCCMS)
author: Other
description: The North Carolina Community Mapping System (NCCMS), Version 1.0 was created by the N.C Department of Environmental Quality (DEQ) to help expand knowledge and understanding of environmental information and permitting related to public health to encourage local outreach and public participation across NC communities. It is an interactive public tool that allows a wide variety of users to access important data about local communities such as community members, academics, advocates and industry. The location of community landfills, septage sites, waste sites, and soil remediation, among other factors, are displayed on a N.C. map based on user queries of specific or general addresses/areas. In addition, a link to an environmental justice tool, the Environmental Health Dashboard, is provided that promotes the understanding of sociodemographic and health characteristics in communities across North Carolina along with related health statistics. The NCCMS is not an exhaustive description of the current environment of North Carolina but rather is intended for use in concert with other tools and methodologies, e.g., EPA’s EJScreen, which is also a resource in the CHORDS Catalog.

title: Monitoring Trends in Burn Severity (MTBS)
author: United States Geological Survey (USGS)
description: Monitoring Trends in Burn Severity (MTBS) is an interagency program that maps the location, extent, and associated burn severity of all large fires (including wildfires, resource benefit fires, and prescribed fires) in the U.S. Thousands of fires have been mapped and the data made available to the public [Picotte, et al, 2020]. The data include large fire perimeter and burn severity for use by the fire science community. Analysis of historical trends in burn severity across the U.S. in 2016 showed that burn area and severity have not changed substantially for most vegetation groups evaluated [Picotte, et al, 2016].

title: OpenStreetMap Database
author: Other
description: The OpenStreetMap (OSM) is a collection of worldwide community contributed or volunteered (crowd-sourced) global map information that includes roads, trails, cafés, railway stations, and much more. New, updated, and complete data are made available each week in both compressed XML and a custom PBF format.

title: NIAID Data Ecosystem Portal
author: National Institute of Health (NIH)
description: The NIAID Data Ecosystem Discovery Portal is a platform developed by the National Institute of Allergy and Infectious Diseases (NIAID) to facilitate the discovery, access, and integration of research data related to infectious diseases, immunology, and other NIAID-supported scientific areas. It is designed to make data more accessible to researchers, supporting collaborations and enabling advances in biomedical research. Key Features 1 Data Discovery: A centralized location to explore datasets, tools, and resources from NIAID-funded projects and initiatives. 2 Metadata Integration: Provides standardized metadata to ensure datasets are searchable and interoperable across studies. 3 Open Access: Many resources on the portal are openly accessible, promoting data sharing and transparency. 4 Community Tools: Features tools for data analysis, visualization, and integration. 5 Support for FAIR Principles: Aligns with the FAIR (Findable, Accessible, Interoperable, and Reusable) principles for data management.

title: The Tropospheric Emissions: Monitoring of Pollution (TEMPO)
author: National Aeronautics and Space Administration (NASA)
description: The Tropospheric Emissions: Monitoring of Pollution (TEMPO) instrument was designed for NASA 's mission to monitor air pollution over North America with high temporal and spatial resolution. It represents a significant advancement in how scientists observe and understand air quality. TEMPO was launched aboard a SpaceX Falcon 9 rocket on April 7, 2023, hosted on the Intelsat 40e satellite. TEMPO is mounted on a commercial communications satellite in geostationary orbit (~35,786 km above the equator), allowing it to continuously observe the same area of the Earth, specifically North America, and collect data in near-real-time.

title: National Health Interview Survey
author: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC)
description: The National Health Interview Survey (NHIS) is a large-scale household interview survey conducted by the National Center for Health Statistics (NCHS), which is part of the CDC in the United States. The NHIS collects data on a broad range of health topics, including: 1)Health Status: Information on chronic diseases, disabilities, mental health, and general physical condition. 2)Access to Health Care: Data on access to health services, health insurance coverage, and barriers to receiving care. 3)Health Behaviors: Questions about lifestyle factors like smoking, alcohol consumption, physical activity, and diet. 4)Utilization of Health Services: Information on how frequently people visit healthcare providers, receive preventive care, and use hospital services. 5)Demographics and Socioeconomic Status: Age, gender, race/ethnicity, education, employment status, income, and other socio-demographic factors.

title: Behavioral Risk Factor Surveillance System (BRFSS)
author: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC)
description: The CDC BRFSS is a collection of health-related surveys regarding health-related risk behaviors, chronic health conditions, and the use of preventative services. The BRFSS data is primarily collected from telephone surveys issued at the state level. BRFSS was established in 1984 with 15 states and now includes all 50 states, three US territories, and the District of Columbia. BRFSS conducts more than 400,000 adult interviews each year and is considered one of the largest continuously conducted health surveys in the world.

title: Modern-Era Retrospective analysis for Research and Applications Version 2 (MERRA-2)
author: National Aeronautics and Space Administration (NASA)
description: A monthly time-averaged 2-dimensional data collection of atmospheric aerosol parameters is provided in the Modern-Era Retrospective analysis for Research and Applications version 2 (MERRA-2). Global model and data assimilations that incorporate satellite-based observations are used to predict monthly aerosol parameters such as column mass density and surface-level mass concentrations of aerosol components, total extinction (and scattering), and aerosol optical thickness, at 550 nm.

title: Yale Center for Earth Observation (YCEO) Global Surface Urban Heat Island (GSUHI) Data, Pixel-Level Composites, Version 5
author: Other
description: The Global Surface Urban Heat Island (GSUHI) displays composite data for surface urban heat islands on a global interactive map based on the intensity of urban clusters. The data, observed from 2003 to 2020, are obtained from satellite-based land surface temperature observations (MODIS) at 300 m resolution, the Landscan urban extent database, and other land cover and elevation data sources.

title: Sonoma Leading Health Indicators
author: Other
description: The Sonoma Leading Health Indicators (SLHI) is a profile of Indicators that highlight major health conditions or events that can compromise health or the healthy development of children. The health indicators are those that contribute most prominently to illness and injury, disability and death for Sonoma County adults and children. The profile includes high-level summaries of data obtained from local and state sources pertaining to leading causes of death for the total population and by age group. The age groups include children pre-natal up to age 1; children 1 to 12 years; children 13 to 17 years; adults 18 to 59 years; and age 60 and above. The leading causes of death are presented for each age group along with Information on significant causes of morbidity including risk factors and behaviors that contribute to morbidity. Indicators of health and healthy development are also available when seeking insight into children’s overall health status.

title: County Health Rankings & Roadmaps
author: Robert Wood Johnson Foundation
description: CHR&R is a U.S. public health initiative that provides county-level data and tools to help communities understand health and equity across the nation. It’s widely used by policymakers, public health practitioners, researchers, leaders, and advocates to assess how different counties compare on conditions that shape health and to support local action. Users can search data by county, state, or ZIP code to explore how one county stacks up against others on dozens of measures. This helps identify strengths, challenges, and disparities in community health conditions. Every year, CHR&R updates its database and releases annual findings report that synthesizes trends across counties and explores national insights into health conditions and disparities.

title: Power Sector Data and Tools (PSDaT)
author: Environmental Protection Agency (EPA)
description: The EPA Power Sector Data and Tools (PSDaT), provided by the Clean Air and Power Division, offers a suite of emissions data and related tools that are organized into five components: The Power Sector Data, Summary Data, Data on Allowances, Environmental Monitoring Data, and Power Sector Modeling. A variety of tools to view, download, and assess data specific to individual components are available and described in each component. The power plant emission data collected by EPA since 1990 are the primary source of data for PSDaT. 1) The Power Sector Data section includes emissions data, e.g., hourly emissions of CO2, NOX, SO2 and mercury, along with facility attribute data, e.g., fuel type, emission control devices. As of 2018, data from approximately 96% of the fossil fuel power plants in the U.S. are represented. 2) The Summary Data component contains summary-level data at varying levels of detail from EPA's power sector programs, such as reports and resources to help understand the data that reflect emission reductions. 3) The Data on Allowances includes allowance allocations, accounts, holdings, and transactions data that are used along with emissions data to determine compliance of regulated sources. 4) The Environmental Monitoring Data component presents data for use in evaluating changes in air quality, atmospheric deposition, and water quality in lakes and streams. 5) The Power Sector Modeling section provides access to Information and documentation on EPA’s power sector modeling resources and regulatory applications.

title: USDA/DOI Wildland Fire Application Information Portal
author: United States Department of Agriculture (USDA)
description: The Wildland Fire Application Information Portal (WFAIP) is a centralized gateway / platform bringing together applications, tools, and services that support federal, state, and local government groups responsible for wildland fire management. It includes operational tools used in planning, responding, tracking, and managing wildland fire and aviation operations. WFAIP also includes access to resources with real-time & historical maps, fire perimeters, weather overlays, etc., such as the National Interagency Fire Center (NIFC) tools.

title: National Contaminant Occurrence Database (NCOD)
author: Environmental Protection Agency (EPA)
description: The National Contaminant Occurrence Database (NCOD) is maintained by the US EPA that offers access to data on the occurrence of both regulated and unregulated drinking water contaminants according to information in the Safe Drinking Water Act (SDWA). They have been collected from 1988 to present and are stored in the NCOD. Data for regulated drinking water contaminants are available from links to four, Six-Year Reviews of national drinking water regulations. The unregulated contaminants data are accessed from links to five, Unregulated Contaminant Monitoring Rule Datasets. The data include measurements of chemical, microbial, and radiological contaminants that support the EPA's regulatory decision-making when reviewing unregulated contaminants that may require future regulation under the SDWA. All of the data help support research and risk assessments and provide transparency through public access to information about drinking-water contaminants.

title: Low-income Energy Affordability Data
author: Other
description: The Low-Income Energy Affordability Data (LEAD) Tool is an interactive, web-based platform developed by the U.S. Department of Energy. Its goal is to help state and local governments, utilities, nonprofits, researchers, and other stakeholders better understand energy burdens and household energy costs for low- and moderate-income (LMI) populations. As per LEAD the definition of energy burden” means the percentage of household income spent on energy costs. Households with 6% or more of their income going to energy are considered to have “high energy burden.”

title: Environmental Quality Index (EQI)
author: Environmental Protection Agency (EPA)
description: The Environmental Quality Index (EQI) is a composite environmental indicator developed by the EPA to summarize overall environmental quality across the US. It combines many environmental variables such as Air, Water, Land, built environment, and Sociodemographic environment into a single index to help researchers study links between environmental conditions and human health outcomes. The primary purpose of EQI is to promote environmental health research to study associations between environmental quality and health outcomes such as mortality, birth outcomes, asthma, cancer, and chronic diseases.

title: OMH Health Profiles by Population
author: Department of Health and Human Services (DHHS)
description: The Health Profiles by Population is an information resource developed by the HHS, Office of Minority Health (OMH). It provides comprehensive profiles of racial and ethnic minority populations in the United States, including: Demographics, Socioeconomic characteristics (education, income, insurance), Health status and outcomes, and social determinants of health. Each profile (e.g., African American, Hispanic/Latino, Asian American, etc.) synthesizes data from multiple federal and non-federal sources. This resource is designed to support: Public health research on disparities, Policy development and evaluation, Program planning and resource allocation, and Education and awareness of minority health issues.

title: Congressional District Health Dashboard (CDHD)
author: Robert Wood Johnson Foundation
description: The Congressional District Health Dashboard (CDHD), released in January 2023, was developed by the Department of Population Health at NYU Grossman School of Medicine, in partnership with Robert Wood Johnson Foundation. As the first-of-its-kind resource, CDHD currently provides reliable data from 2019 - 2023 that represent 40 measures of health and factors affecting health for all congressional districts across the U.S. The measures, or metrics, represent five key areas: Health Behaviors, Social and Economic Factors, Physical Environment, Health Outcomes, and Clinical Care. Data for metrics such as air pollution, life expectancy, physical inactivity, diabetes rates, uninsured, and children in poverty, are available for use by policymakers, advocates, community members, and members of Congress for assessing strengths and challenges in their regions and help drive positive change at the district, state, and national level. Snapshots of results from searches of data from all 40 metrics based on a selected state or Congressional District are displayed along with corresponding national averages. The results can be saved as pdf or png files of the map and/or bar plots. Links to demographics and information about Senators and other representatives of the selected district are also included on the results page. Options to view data for individual metrics on district maps or as bar graphs based on demographics are also available. In addition, data for individual metrics, e.g., unemployment, can be displayed for multiple districts as compared to the national average.

title: Water Quality Portal (WQP)
author: Environmental Protection Agency (EPA)
description: The Water Quality Portal (WQP), maintained by the National Water Quality Monitoring Council, is the premiere online database of discrete water-quality data from over 400 state, federal, tribal, and local agencies for more than 2.7 million sites across the United States. It is a cooperative service that integrates water-quality data from the U.S. Geological Survey, National Water Information System and the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency, Water Quality Exchange (formerly the STORET database), a data warehouse for water quality, biological, and physical data. An online query form is provided to search the available data and download results as comma delimited CSV files. The information may include locations where samples were collected or chemical (analytical data of collected samples), physical, and/or biological characteristics of surface water and/or groundwater depending on the user's input.

title: Air Toxics Screening Assessment
author: Environmental Protection Agency (EPA)
description: The EPA Air Toxics Screening Assessment Tool (ATSA), also referred to as AirToxScreen, offers screening-level estimates from risk assessments for cancer and other serious health effects related to breathing outdoor pollutants over many years. It provides updated data and risk analyses on an annual basis and was designed to guide users in the pursuit of more detailed data associated with air toxics risk. State, local and tribal air agencies, EPA, and the public can use AirToxScreen to easily identify existing as well as emerging issues, such as which air toxics, emission sources, and/or places may need further study to better understand risks. Screening data at the national, state, county, and census block levels from assessment of air toxics data from specified years are available for download. A mapping tool that displays cancer risks, emissions data, and other AirToxScreen data on a map of the United States is also available. Map search tools are used to zoom to areas of specific interest by selecting census blocks to view summary level information. Links are also provided to supporting data and files, including other web pages and data sources, that offer more information about screening results.

title: EPA-Smart Growth
author: Environmental Protection Agency (EPA)
description: Smart Growth refers to a set of principles and tools promoted by the EPA to help communities develop in ways that are economically strong, environmentally sustainable, and socially equitable. Smart Growth focuses on creating walkable, mixed-use, transit-supported neighborhoods that reduce sprawl, protect natural resources, and improve quality of life.   Smart Growth have core 10 principles and have strategies that includes:  Transit-oriented development, infill development and redevelopment, green infrastructure (rain gardens, permeable pavement), form-based zoning codes, complete Streets design.

title: Disaster Research Response (DR2) Resources Portal
author: National Institute of Health (NIH)
description: The Disaster Research Response (DR2) Resources Portal, hosted by the National Institute of Environmental Health Sciences (NIEHS) and funded by NIH, is a comprehensive, publicly accessible repository designed to support human health research in the aftermath of disasters and public health emergencies. It offers more than 500 curated tools and resources, including surveys, study protocols, consent forms, data dictionaries, and more,  to facilitate rapid and effective research deployment. DR2 resources are categorized by disaster type, including floods, fires, hurricanes, and chemical spills, enabling researchers to find relevant tools tailored to specific events. DR2 also has a dedicated section that provides tools and protocols related to COVID-19 research, including surveys, data dictionaries, and study protocols.

title: Neighborhood Atlas
author: National Institute of Health (NIH)
description: The Neighborhood Atlas developed by the University of Wisconsin, Madison School of Medicine and Public Health is a national geospatial dataset and mapping tool designed to measure and visualize socioeconomic disadvantage at the neighborhood level across the US. It provides Area Deprivation Index (ADI), a composite measure of neighborhood disadvantage. The ADI is a composite socioeconomic index measuring neighborhood disadvantage (the “social exposome”). It combines 17 indicators across domains such as: Income, Education, Employment,  Housing quality etc. It provides ranked scores (national percentiles and state deciles) to compare neighborhoods.

title: Science Collaborative for Health Disparities and Artificial intelligence Reduction of Errors Repository
author: National Institute of Health (NIH)
description: Science Collaborative for Health Disparities and Artificial Intelligence Reduction of Errors (SCHARE) Repository, managed by the NIH, National Institute on Minority Health and Health Disparities (NIMHD), is a cloud-based population science data platform meant to provide centralized resources (datasets, tools, workspaces) to support research into health disparities, health care delivery, and health outcomes, using transparent, fit-for-purpose AI and ML methods. The repository holds population science data, place-based data, behavioral data, as well as cloud-computing tools to support paradigm-shifting work in health disparities and whole-person health. It is designed to use core common data elements to encourage interoperability and data aggregation as well as reduce AI bias to improve the accuracy and reliability of AI tools to minimize errors. All registered users have access to data within the repository at varying privacy and security levels based on controlled access. SCHARE has also developed initiatives like “Think-a-Thons”, webinars to help people learn AI / cloud computing terminology, develop research ideas, and form collaborations.

title: Restoration and Protection Screening Database
author: Environmental Protection Agency (EPA)
description: The RPS Tool is an online application designed to explore data in the national RPS Indicator Database and allow users to screen data collected for HUC12 subwatersheds (the smallest standard unit for identification) mapped across the contiguous U.S. Based on user input, results from each screening run are presented in a table of index scores (Ecological, Stressor, Social or Integrated) in rank order of each index, and in customizable graphic bubble plots and HUC12 statewide map displays. Maps can be used to view single indicator values for any index or indicator and saved as JPG files. A user can choose to run a screening on all watersheds in a geographic area or just a subset (e.g., or all HUC12s with impaired waters) based on hundreds of social, ecological, and stressor indicators at the HUC12 scale. The most relevant indicators for a selected screening can also be selected for weight purposes. In addition to the web application, the RPS Tool is also available a custom-coded Excel spreadsheet that performs all RPS index calculations and generates RPS outputs as rank-ordered tables, maps and bubble plots. The RPS Indicator Database is updated annually.

title: FEMA Geospatial Resource Center Data Catalog
author: Federal Emergency Management Agency (FEMA)
description: The FEMA Geospatial Resource Center (FGRC) provides easy access to a wide range of disaster response resources needed before, during, and after disasters occur. All data and information are maintained in a secure environment and are available to the public and to decision-makers at the federal, state, and local level. Individual datasets from governmental, private sector, and volunteer agency partners, including HHS, Census, NASA, and many others, are accessible in the FGRC Content Gallery. More than 600 resources, e.g., datasets, maps, and applications, are organized by Hazard Specific Categories such as disaster types, e.g., Floods, Hurricanes, Tornadoes, Wildfires, Earthquakes, Snow and Winter Weather, and Other Disasters. The resources for each disaster type are further organized by Hazard Overview, Applications, Data & Imagery, State & Local Resources, and/or Historic Resources.  Access to emergency management resources is also provided for each U.S. state and territory by the State GIS/Emergency Management Resources application. Other resources available in the FGRC Content Gallery include Predictive Models, Live Feeds, Incident Phases, and Base Data.

title: CDC National Wastewater Surveillance System (NWSS)
author: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC)
description: NWSS is a U.S. public-health surveillance program that monitors pathogens in wastewater to detect community-level disease trends. It detects viral genetic material (RNA/DNA) from pathogens such as SARS-CoV-2, influenza A, RSV, mpox, and others, providing an early warning signal for outbreaks before increases appear in clinical testing data. Wastewater monitoring is an effective model that works because infected individuals shed pathogens in stool and other bodily fluids that enter sewer systems, allowing population-level detection without individual testing.

title: Minority Health Index Explorer (MHIE)
author: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC)
description: The MHIE is a geospatial dashboard that allows users to visualize and analyze the Minority Health Social Vulnerability Index across the United States. Its primary purpose is to help public health professionals and policymakers identify minority communities that are more vulnerable to health emergencies and adverse health outcomes. It covers health indicators such as: Healthcare access, Chronic disease prevalence, Race and ethnicity distribution, Language proficiency, Socioeconomic factors, and Household characteristics.

author: US EPA
description:

If you have questions about the underlying data stored here, please contact tri.help@epa.gov. If you have questions or recommendations related to this metadata entry and extracted data, please contact the CAFE Data Management team at: climatecafe@bu.edu.
"EPA has been collecting Toxics Release Inventory (TRI) data since 1987. The "Basic Plus" data files include ten file types that collectively contain all of the data fields from the TRI Reporting Form R and Form A. The files themselves are in tab-delimited .txt format and then compressed into a .zip file.
1a: Facility, chemical, releases and other waste management summary information
1b: Chemical activities and uses
2a: On- and off-site disposal, treatment, energy recovery, and recycling information; non-production-related waste managed quantities; production/activity ratio information; and source reduction activities
2b: Detailed on-site waste treatment methods and efficiency
3a: Transfers off site for disposal and further waste management
3b: Transfers to Publicly Owned Treatment Works (POTWs) (RY1987 - RY2010)
3c: Transfers to Publicly Owned Treatment Works (POTWs) (RY2011 - Present)
4: Facility information
5: Optional information on source reduction, recycling and pollution control (RY2005 - Present)
6: Additional miscellaneous and optional information (RY2010 - Present) Quantities of dioxin and dioxin-like compounds are reported in grams, while all other chemicals are reported in pounds.
This webpage contains the most recent versions of all TRI data files; facilities may revise previous years' TRI submissions if necessary, and any such changes will be reflected in these files. For this reason, data contained in these files may differ from data used to construct the TRI National Analysis."
[Quote from https://www.epa.gov/toxics-release-inventory-tri-program/tri-basic-plus-data-files-calendar-years-1987-present]

The Geospatial Research, Analysis & Services Program (GRASP) maintains the CDC/ATSDR SVI to help public health officials and local planners better prepare for and respond to emergency events with the goal of decreasing human suffering, economic loss, and health inequities." Advanced users can download the full EJScreen datasets as raw data files. The available data includes the EJScreen as compared to the state or nation. They are available for download at the block group or tract level resolution in geodatabase (.gdb) or comma separated values (.csv) formats. title: Extracted Data from: Climate and Economic Justice Screening Tool 2.0 (CEJST / Justice40 2.0)
author: The Council on Environmental Quality (CEQ)
description:
This submission includes publicly available data extracted in its original form. Please reference the Related Publication listed here for source and citation information.

This tool is called the Climate and Economic Justice Screening Tool. The tool uses datasets that are indicators of burdens in eight categories: climate change, energy, health, housing, legacy pollution, transportation, water and wastewater, and workforce development. The tool uses this information to identify communities that are experiencing these burdens. These are the communities that are disadvantaged because they are marginalized by underinvestment and overburdened by pollution.

CEQ will update the tool, after reviewing public feedback, research, and the availability of new data.

Version 2.0 Release update - Dec 20, 2024 New & Improved Added the low income burden to American Samoa, Guam, the Mariana Islands, and the U.S. Virgin Islands Tracts in these territories that are completely surrounded by disadvantaged tracts and exceed an adjusted low income threshold are now considered disadvantaged Additionally, census tracts in these four Territories are now considered disadvantaged if they meet the low income threshold only, because these Territories are not included in the nationally-consistent datasets on environmental and climate burdens used in the tool Updated the data in the workforce development category to the Census Decennial 2020 data for the U.S. territories of Guam, Virgin Islands, Northern Mariana Islands, and American Samoa Made improvements to the low income burden to better identify college students before they are excluded from that burden’s percentile Census tracts that were disadvantaged under version 1.0 continue to be considered as disadvantaged in version 2.0 Technical Fixes For tracts that have water boundaries, e.g. coastal or island tracts, the water boundaries are now excluded from the calculation to determine if a tract is 100% surrounded by disadvantaged census tracts User Interface Improvements Added the ability to search by census tract ID The basemap has been updated to use a free, open source map

If you have questions or recommendations related to this metadata entry and extracted data, please contact the CAFE Data Management team at: climatecafe@bu.edu

"The Emissions & Generation Resource Integrated Database (eGRID) is a comprehensive inventory of environmental attributes of electric power systems. The preeminent source of air emission data for the electric power sector, eGRID is based on available plant-specific data for all U.S. electricity generating plants that provide power to the electric grid and report data to the U.S. government. eGRID uses data from the Energy Information Administration (EIA) Forms EIA-860 and EIA-923 and EPA’s Clean Air Power Sector Program Data. Emission data from EPA are carefully integrated with generation data from EIA to produce useful values like pounds of emissions per megawatt-hour of electricity generation (lb/MWh), which allows direct comparison of the environmental attributes of electricity generation. eGRID also provides aggregated data by state, U.S. total, and by three different sets of electric grid boundaries (i.e., balancing authority area, North American Electric Reliability Corporation (NERC) region, and eGRID subregion)." [Quote from https://www.epa.gov/egrid/frequent-questions-about-egrid#egrid1]

author: Environmental Protection Agency
description:

"The Environmental and Climate Justice Program (ECJ Program), created by the Inflation Reduction Act (IRA), provides funding for financial and technical assistance to carry out environmental and climate justice activities to benefit disadvantaged communities.

EPA has created the EPA Disadvantaged Community Environmental and Climate Justice Program map to assist potential applicants seeking to identify whether a community is disadvantaged for the purposes of implementing the ECJ Program.

The EPA Disadvantaged Communities Environmental and Climate Justice program map includes the following components:

EPA IRA Disadvantaged Communities 1.0 map EPA IRA Disadvantaged Communities 2.0 map Any area of American Samoa, Guam, the Commonwealth of the Northern Mariana Islands, or the U.S. Virgin Islands

The EPA IRA Disadvantaged Communities maps combines multiple datasets that individually can be used to determine whether a community is disadvantaged for the purposes of implementing programs under the IRA. All data sets are assigned values at the Census block group level.

The criteria and associated datasets used in the maps are:

Any census tract that is included as disadvantaged in the Climate and Economic Justice Screening Tool (CEJST) Any census block group at or above the 90th percentile for any of EJScreen’s Supplemental Indexes when compared to the nation or state, and/or any of the following geographic areas within the Tribal lands category in EJScreen: Alaska Native Allotments Alaska Native Villages American Indian Reservations American Indian Off-reservation Trust Lands Oklahoma Tribal Statistical Areas

The EPA IRA Disadvantaged Communities 1.0 map uses data from EJScreen version 2.2. The EPA IRA Disadvantaged Communities 2.0 map uses data from EJScreen version 2.3.

To further assist applicants, EPA has provided the underlying data for the map"

[Quote from https://www.epa.gov/environmentaljustice/inflation-reduction-act-disadvantaged-communities-map]

title: Extracted Data From: Climate Resilience Evaluation and Awareness Tool
author: Environmental Protection Agency
description:
This submission includes publicly available data extracted in its original form. Please reference the Related Publication listed here for source and citation information

If you have questions about the source data, please contact the EPA Creating Resilient Water Utilities (CRWU) (CRWUhelp@epa.gov). If you have questions about metadata, please contact CAFE (climatecafe@bu.edu)

"EPA's Creating Resilient Water Utilities (CRWU) initiative provides drinking water, wastewater, and stormwater (water sector) utilities with practical tools, training, and technical assistance needed to increase resilience to climate change. CRWU’s Climate Resilience Evaluation and Awareness Tool (CREAT) and interactive maps (Storm Surge Inundation Map, CREAT Climate Scenarios Projection Map, and the Streamflow Projections Map), provide utilities with pre-processed data to aid in climate-based decision-making. To increase accessibility for geospatial users, CRWU provided these datasets in a zipped file geodatabase format.
The data provided here are projected changes in climate conditions. These data and additional projections associated with the same scenarios are available in CREAT.
The scenarios used to present the range of model projections for each grid cell on the map were:
'Hot/Dry' scenario based on the average of the five climate models that project hotter and drier conditions compared to the other model projections; ‘Central’ scenario based on the average of the five climate models that project changes in the middle of the distribution of projections; and 'Warm/Wet’ scenario based on the average of the five climate models that project less warming but increased precipitation relative to other models. Changes in annual temperature, annual precipitation, and 100-year storm intensity are provided as increases (positive values) or decreases (negative values) relative to a baseline of observed climate at the same location from 1981-2010. Each projection includes changes in temperature and precipitation averaged over two 20-year time periods: '2035 period' from 2026-2045 and '2060 period' from 2051-2070.
The number of hot days associated with each climate station are reported as the number of days per year that have temperatures over 100 °F following the temperature increases projected by the same models. Climate station data were built upon the dataset of the Global Historical Climatology Network (GHCN)-Daily. The dataset is being maintained at the National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration’s National Climatic Data Centre (NCDC).
Additional information on the selection of scenarios and the sources of data used can be found in the CREAT 3.1 Methodology Guide. Models used were drawn from the Coupled Model Intercomparison Project Phase 5 (CMIP5) dataset.
To explore data for a location or to download data from this map, please visit the CRWU Data Access page." [Quote from: https://epa.maps.arcgis.com/apps/MapSeries/index.html?appid=3805293158d54846a29f750d63c6890e]

title: Extracted Data From: Environmental Quality Index (EQI)
author: Environmental Protection Agency
description:
This submission includes publicly available data extracted in its original form. Please reference the Related Publication listed here for source and citation information

"The Environmental Quality Index (EQI) presents data in five domains: air, water, land, built, and sociodemographic environments to provide a county-by-county snapshot of overall environmental quality across the entire U.S. The EQI helps researchers better understand how health outcomes relate to cumulative environmental exposures that typically are viewed in isolation.

Because the air we breathe, the water we drink, the food we eat, and the buildings around us all play a part in our overall wellbeing, researchers can use the EQI to investigate and highlight associations between environmental quality and a given health concern. By expanding research beyond singular routes of exposure and pollutants, the EQI helps provide a clearer picture of the relationship between the environment and health. To support this scientific exploration, EPA has made the EQI and its many variables open and accessible to the public. You can learn more about the EQI under downloads below.

Since the release of the EQI 2000-2005, researchers have published multiple studies examining the relationship between overall environmental quality and health outcomes, including preterm birth, mortality, cancer incidence, asthma prevalence, physical inactivity and obesity, infant mortality, and pediatric multiple sclerosis. You can find EQI related publications below.

EQI data can also be used by ecologists, economists, and other researchers to address questions about environmental quality.

At its current county-level scale, the EQI may not reveal environmental injustices seen at the local community level. However, it does highlight counties experiencing an increased burden of environmental impacts, and the domain-specific indices enable counties to assess the drivers of poor environmental quality in their counties, which could provide a foundation for targeted support to address health concerns tied to environmental quality.

Because modifications were made to the updated EQI 2006-2010, direct comparisons between EQI 2000-2005 and EQI 2006-2010 should not be made. The two indices should not be examined as being continuous over time (e.g., if a study period covers 2004-2007, only one of the indices should be chosen or study population should be stratified by time period matched to the appropriate EQI). Furthermore, the EQI was developed for research purposes and is not meant to be a diagnostic tool. The EQI would be useful to identify potential areas of concern for counties to target future research, but it should not be used to target regulatory purposes."

[Quote from https://www.epa.gov/healthresearch/environmental-quality-index-eqi]

If you have questions about the source data, please contact the Environmental Protection Agency (ferster.aaron@epa.gov, https://ecomments.epa.gov/eqi/). If you have questions about metadata, please contact CAFE (climatecafe@bu.edu)

title: Extracted Data From: Environmental Justice Index
author: CDC Agency for Toxic Substances and Disease Registry Place and Health - Geospatial Research, Analysis, and Services Program (GRASP)
description:
This submission includes publicly available data extracted in its original form. Please reference the Related Publication listed here for source and citation information

If you have questions about the source data, please contact the CDC Agency for Toxic Substances and Disease Registry Place and Health - Geospatial Research, Analysis, and Services Program (GRASP) (eji_coordinator@cdc.gov). If you have questions about this metadata entry, please contact CAFE (climatecafe@bu.edu)

"Environmental justice means the just treatment and meaningful involvement of all people, regardless of income, race, color, national origin, Tribal affiliation, or disability, in agency decision-making and other Federal activities that affect human health and the environment so that people:

Are fully protected from disproportionate and adverse human health and environmental effects (including risks) and hazards, including those related to climate change, the cumulative impacts of environmental and other burdens, and the legacy of racism or other structural or systemic barriers

Have equitable access to a healthy, sustainable, and resilient environment in which to live, play, work, learn, grow, worship, and engage in cultural and subsistence practices

The Environmental Justice Index uses data from the U.S. Census Bureau, the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency, the U.S. Mine Safety and Health Administration, the U.S. Geological Survey, OpenStreetMap, the U.S. Department of Transportation, and the U.S. Centers for Disease Control and Prevention to rank the cumulative impacts of environmental injustice on health for every census tract. Census tracts are subdivisions of counties for which the Census collects statistical data. The EJI ranks each tract on 36 environmental, social, and health factors and groups them into three overarching modules and ten different domains." [Quote from https://www.atsdr.cdc.gov/place-health/php/eji/index.html]

title: Extracted Data From: Inventory of US GHG Emissions and Sinks
author: U.S. Environmental Protection Agency Office of Atmospheric Programs (MC-6207A)
description:
This submission includes publicly available data extracted in its original form. Please reference the Related Publication listed here for source and citation information

"EPA develops an annual report called the Inventory of U.S. Greenhouse Gas Emissions and Sinks (Inventory), that tracks U.S. greenhouse gas emissions and sinks by source, economic sector, and greenhouse gas going back to 1990. EPA has prepared the Inventory of U.S. Greenhouse Gas Emissions and Sinks since the early 1990s. This annual report, provides a comprehensive accounting of total greenhouse gas emissions for all man-made sources in the United States, including carbon dioxide removal from the atmosphere by “sinks,” (e.g., through the uptake of carbon and storage in forests, vegetation, and soils) from management of lands in their current use or as lands are converted to other uses. The gases covered by the Inventory include carbon dioxide, methane, nitrous oxide, hydrofluorocarbons, perfluorocarbons, sulfur hexafluoride, and nitrogen trifluoride.

The national greenhouse gas inventory is submitted to the United Nations in accordance with the Framework Convention on Climate Change and also the Paris Agreement. In preparing the annual emissions and sinks inventory report, EPA collaborates with hundreds of experts representing more than a dozen U.S. government agencies, academic institutions, industry associations, consultants and environmental organizations. EPA also collects greenhouse gas emissions data from individual facilities and suppliers of certain fossil fuels and industrial gases through the Greenhouse Gas Reporting Program." [Quote from https://www.epa.gov/ghgemissions/inventory-us-greenhouse-gas-emissions-and-sinks]

If you have questions about the source data, please contact the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency Office of Atmospheric Programs (MC-6207A) (ghgreporting@epa.gov, https://www.epa.gov/ghgemissions/forms/contact-us-about-greenhouse-gas-emissions).
